stats.Total Time: 40 minutes (or about 2 hours if making the bread)
stats.Cook Time: 20 minutes (or 1½ hours if making the bread)

comments.commentBody: Interesting recipe for the lamb. Tried it using 1 pound of ground lamb as finding a 1 lb boneless leg of lamb was not feasible. Served with Naan bread.  Good blend and made a good burger. Probably very good with sliced lamb,and I may use the rub  next time I cook a whole boneless leg.

Costco has a 2# boneless leg of lamb that can be cut in half.  Freeze half of the leg.  If you have access to a real butcher as opposed to prepackaged supermarket meat, they should be able to give you 1 lb of boneless leg.  Even my Safeway meat cutter will pretty much give me what I ask for.

Per the suggestion below I obtained a 3 lb boneless leg from Costco - the smallest they had (and a beautiful piece of meat, btw).  After trimming I used half for this and upsized slightly.  Results were delicious and very much like the original dish.  Vacuum froze other half to make again in a few months.

Best not to call these a “burger” else it’s easy to misunderstand and use ground lamb. Needs to be strips of spiced and grilled lamb meat. Made correctly these are one of my favorite street food dishes in Chinese cuisine.
Made this with the spice mixture using ground lamb.  It was good - served with warm Naan.  Would use the spice mixture as a rub for a butterfly leg of lamb.
Did you add the cornstarch to the ground lamb?
I think the spice mixture sounds divine and have the same cornstarch question.
I am using ground lamb and if here is a reason to use the cornstarch, I am not.  Will update with the result.

The nearest Xi'an is often my first food stop on my many trips to NY. Now if only I could translate these flavors into the dumplings with that spicy, oily red broth, all my problems would be solved (mostly).
@Dave T Genuinely believe buying a jar of their chili crisp oil is the answer! They ship it! And sometimes they sell it on Amazon too.

The lamb was delicious, great flavor combo.  I sauteed all of the veggies, including the onions and chilis, before adding the lamb, because I didn't like the idea of nearly raw onions and jalepeno.  The result was less browning, but a lovely blend of pungent flavors after I added the cumin and chili powder.   The "buns", however, were a total dud.  Dense hockey pucks that were raw inside and inedible.  I would recommend using Meera Sodha's recipe for naan on this website; its amazing.

For the spice mixture Chile powder I used gochugaru and cayenne. I also pre-marinated the lamb in some baking soda, soy sauce, msg, and shaoxing (small amounts of all for ~2hrs). Only other sub was jalapeno peppers. The meat came out wonderfully spiced, tender, and moist. Definitely a keeper. I'm not sure what, but I think the buns could benefit from a topping, something light, maybe an acid based slaw to add some crunch and freshness.
I had to knead the dough for twice as long, ten minutes, to get it to be smooth & supple.
HORRIBLE! Cumin completely overpowered this dish. What a waste of time and ingredients!
I used thinly sliced lamb that I got from the Asian market in my small midwestern city.

Thought the recipe was way too heavy on the ground cumin, to the extent that I couldn't really identify the meat beneath all that cumin.  If I were to make these again, I'd reduce the cumin by half.  

Also, I used normal-sized burger buns in lieu of making the buns.  I found that the amount of cooked meat was way too much for normal burger buns.  I could have easily made twice as many sandwiches.

Also, not being a fan of raw hot peppers or raw red onion, I cooked both with the lamb.
To make this vegetarian, I'm thinking of substituting vegan chicken for the meat and making it a shawarma style Middle Eastern style dish.
Considering doing this in a cast iron tray in my pizza oven at very high heat… thoughts?

Be sure the wok/skillet is VERY hot, as the lamb will release a good bit of fat.  I made mine in a wok and had a hard time getting the lamb to brown, so I finished it under a broiler for some texture.  Next time I'll use a large skillet with more surface area.  

That said, the flavors were great (as always, lamb needs a generous amount of salt) and we loved it paired with a simple shirazi salad (not Asian of course, but still a nice complement).
Has anyone tried kneading the dough with the hook attachment of a stand mixer (instead of hand kneading)?

I have used ground lamb to make Merguez for a very long time.
The recipe for it has more spices than this recipe and I suspect tastes better.

I did not make the bread. Not sure why anyone would, ha. 

That said, I loved it. I wasn’t brave enough to add all that cumin at the end. I did about half. Felt excessive, trusted my instincts. Maybe next time I’ll add it all. Enjoyed it on Hawaiian buns with feta and minty yogurt and killer homemade herby fries.
I don't wrap food in plastic, rather close it in a tupperware or in a towel

Lamb is delicious.  But as others have mentioned, the buns are a DUD.  Something is definitely off in the proportion of ingredients, the instructions, or both.  For example the ratio of water to flour is WAY off - the dough is a dry, dense mess.  And the timing of the instructions doesn't make sense (2 hours if making the buns, when all activities including frying as written take only 40 minutes??)
The cumin tended to be very overpowering so I tempered it with light soy sauce along with XO sauce for some earthly flavor.
This is for people that don’t like lamb.  I only used 2 T cumin and it was overwhelming.  Didn’t try the rolls.
Yes, corn starch to ground lamb

Meh. I like cumin, but this was too much, as was the salt. The idea is great, though, and it comes together quickly. It would be good with half the cumin. Next time I'll serve with tzatziki and harissa sauces on pita.

title: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: The best sandwich is the simplist one. A few years ago a neighbor gifted us a couple of large ripe heirloom tomatoes. I placed a few slices on white bread, added a little salt, dabbed a decent smear of mayonniase and took a bite. Wow! It was a revelation that something so simple can taste so good. Tried sprinkling a little parmesan but it killed the pureness of the tomato. I truly believe a well constructed tomato sandwich can stand up to a cucumber sandwich any day of the week.

While the flavors were good, it was much more oily than our family cares for. Mayo on the bread to toast, more mayo on the sandwich, oil to cook the halloumi, dressing on the arugula, then remaining dressing drizzled over it. I'm not sure I'd dress the arugula if I were to make it again. I might also consider simply toasting the bread.
@Maura agreed - it was overly and needlessly greasy and heavy. Not making again.
@Maura you could also try grilling the halloumi rather than fry in oil.

Placing tomatoes directly against the bread changes its texture by making it moist. The riper the tomato, the wetter the bread will become, especially if the sandwich is packed to be eaten later. The solution is to place dry ingredients against the bread slices. Therefore, the order in this sandwich should be: bread-halloumi-tomato-arugula-bread.

This is why the mayo and oil is important - mayo and cheese provide a barrier to keep the bread from getting soggy.  Whenever you make a sandwich always put the cheese on one side and the mayo & mustard on the other and the juicy fillers like tomato and meat in the middle to keep the bread dry.
I switched out the halloumi for bacon and it was delicious!
@Kristy360 — try the halloumi! It’s even better. Or, you know, have both (heh heh).

I want to make this over the next few hot days. I have a question: in the ingredients for the garlic pepper mayonnaise, I don’t see any pepper. Do I just add my usual ground black peppercorns to taste or do you have a specific type of pepper in mind?
Thank you

The instructions in the recipe say to season to taste with salt and pepper. I'd go freshly ground, personally. I think if the recipe-writers had a specific pepper in mind, they'd have said. That's my guess.

Added some Aleppo pepper to the garlic mayo spread to give it amazing depth without extra heat. Have made it twice and found the greens need a very small amount of dressing. I also found draining the salted tomatoes over a bowl/sink helps to concentrate the flavor and make the tomatoes just the right amount of juicy. Have also added pickled red onions or banana peppers for an additional flavor profile. So good any way, will definitely make again.
What an awesome sandwich! I used olive oil to toast the bread and did not make the dressing for the arugula due to some people saying it was too oily. Great suggestion!
Halloumi can be hard to find so I substitute Queso De Freir.  It's very inexpensive and has a bit less sodium than the Halloumi.  Same end results - glorious and squeaky.
If you made a different sandwich, why post about it? Just eat your sandwich!
I’m pregnant, which means I go through hyperfixation meals for several weeks. This might last me the rest of the pregnancy. It’s so good. Used ciabatta instead of sourdough.
If you read the instructions, you will see that you season the Mayo with salt and freshly ground black pepper.
@Dan Not in my version.

I made this sandwich using all the ingredients and following the directions exactly, with the following exceptions only:
- Light Duke’s mayo (don’t hate lol)
- Power greens subbed for arugula (I had them on hand)
- I added red pepper flakes to the Greek dressing for the salad (I love heat)
- I used pickled red onions because yes

I thought it was delicious. 

Would I maybe use less mayo next time? Sure. Might I stack it differently next time? Quite possibly. Add pickles? Yum. Is less often more? Yup. And so on. 

This sandwich, as the recipe dictates and as I bowdlerized it, was fabulous.

Much ado about little. I’m going to toast bread in the toaster (wow!) and proceed to make a BLT subbing grilled halloumi for B and arugula for L (maybe dressed, or not) and good old Hellman’s. The T I’m leaving. Some things just don’t need to be that fussy.

Only toasted bread in toaster to avoid oiliness. Made sure to sandwich tomatoes in middle of sandwich to avoid sogginess. Would consider adding honey to halloumi next time to add some sweetness to contrast savory

My husband said this was one of the best sandwiches he’s ever had. I’d opt for a bit less oil or mayo or dressing next time - it was pretty sloppy. Also we put the pickles IN the sandwich. Maybe next time I’ll pickle the red onions first for extra acidity/ crunch. We used a gorgeous fresh cob of sourdough. So yum.

Incredible sandwich that does not take an hour.   We scraped some time off by toasting the bread in the toaster without sacrificing flavor.

Yum!!!!!

responding to Rafael's suggestion of tomato sandwich.
Here in North Carolina, we call it a "mater" sandwich which is a nice white bread, Duke's mayo on both slides, giant fat ripe tomatoes that you salt and pepper.
you can't beat it

This was delicious. Next time I will grill the halloumi (on the pannini grill) as it came out a little too chew/ rubbery this way for my taste.   My husband doesn't like halloumi so I substituted fresh mozzarella and that worked nicely.
Nothing beats a clasic tomato sandwich.... delicious ripe tomato, mayo, salt on good bread. Less is more.

Flavors were great - would make again! 
I read the comments beforehand regarding greasiness and decided not to dress the arugula. There is enough tanginess coming from the other elements that the arugula does not need to be dressed. We did toast the bread with mayo, which was yummy but probably not strictly necessary. Toasting in toaster would lighten it up — just depends on what you’re looking for in a sandwich experience.
OMG I usually think mayo is the grossest thing to have in the fridge, but I made this sandwich completely as written. I may divorce my husband so I can marry it.

Am I missing something here? The recipe is for 4 sandwiches, apparently 1/2 per person. Yet the nutritional info is for 18 servings. Is that a typo and it should instead read 8 servings? That presumably would more than double the calories, fat , sodium etc shown per serving.
Halloumi is one of my favorite cheeses and this is a great way to serve it.  Also love that the recipe builds in a side salad.

This is outstanding for a summer evening. I am Greek and I would not substitute Feta for Haloumi as some have suggested. Feta is an entirely different taste spectrum, and it can’t be fried. Haloumi is easy to find. This not very ’oily’, the amount of mayo and oil used for 4 big sandwiches is not too much at all. Try to get the best tomatoes you can, that’s key. And be generous with the cheese!
Really delicious and perfect for dinner during a heat wave. Kid doesn’t like halloumi so I made them one with pan fried tofu. Everyone enjoyed it.

My groc store that reliably carries arugula and halloumi was out of both. I used the idea to make a paneer and tomato chalupa instead. For the garlic-pepper mayo I used chaat masala spice mix. Added finely chopped mint to the mayo. I had a bagged “Asian salad mix” that I used instead of the arugula.

We've had these 2 days in a row now (had to use up the halloumi!). We followed the recipe to a T first, and it was good but super messy. Today we just used a little plain mayo and a squeeze of lemon and it was outstanding! I found all the oil yesterday blunted the flavors. Today the sourdough, tomato, and arugula were allowed to shine. This "dumbed down" version is definitely going into our summer rotation.
Added fresh pressed garlic to mayo instead of garlic powder. So much better than garlic powder. (To answer question below - S&P added in directions but not listed)

I thought to myself “I mean I like halloumi… I like arugula … I like tomatoes… but is this sandwich really good enough for the nyt to post a recipe for it?” The answer is YES. It’s perfect. No swaps, no notes.
This sandwich eats like a burger. Delicious!
The flavors are fantastic, but next time I will toast the bread without mayo. Just a tad too greasy, and not necessary.

This was the best sandwich I’ve had in years! When I read the recipe I thought it had too many steps and I was tempted to skip most of them (like spreading mayonnaise on the bread and toasting it or adding dressing to the argula) but I am so glad I followed the recipe as written because the end result is a delicious, filling, restaurant quality sandwich that is so easy to make at home! Can’t wait to make this again!
Absolutely delicious. Don’t change a thing. In no way does this take an hour to prepare, though. At most it was 30 minutes and I was prepping side dishes at the same time.
Way too salty.  Too much dressing for 5 oz arugula. Notice the halloumi slices in the pictured sandwich is about 1/4” slice, not 1/2” per directions. The larger slice overtook the sandwich.

The halloumi needs to be sliced really thin to get crispy to replace the bacon.  !/2 " was way to thick IMHO.  The mayo needed lots more pepper to ramp it up.  I also did not like the mayo fried bread.  Next time I will toast in the toaster.

For me, this is a better idea than recipe. Too salty, heavy, and unbalanced as written. We dry fried the halloumi and used a small amount of the lemon mayo inside the sandwich along with thin sliced avocado. Cut the halloumi 1/4 inch. Not sure why you would need more oil on the arugula? We popped these in the panini press but would be fine with normal toast as well.
Already a summer favorite. Waiting for August heirlooms..
I added gochugaru to the mayo for a subtle heat. I pickled a jalapeño with some added Worcestershire for more umami. Toasting the bread on the panin press made a world of difference. Excellent.
I made a version of this where I grilled all of the ingredients (except the arugula & mayo), plus red pepper, and it was one of the best things I’ve ever eaten. Perfect summer sandwich.
AMAZING sandwich.  Toast bread in toaster.  No need for mayo.
I made some beetroot hommus and used that in the sandwich. It’s a winner
I had this with regular toast, no oil on the arugula, avocado, and shallot instead of onion because it was what I had. So good for something so simple!
instant classic veggie sandwich

Love this! Just yesterday I was wondering what to use in place of bacon for my favorite summertime sandwich. HLT is the new BLT! I made this with local tomatoes and skipped the extra dressing. A winner. :)
I turned this into a salad with chickpeas and sourdough croutons and I don’t think I’ve ever made a better decision (threw some olives in too)
Great sandwich. I skipped adding oil to the arugula like some other people suggested and used less mayo. Also thinner slices of cheese. I think adding basil or roasted red peppers would be great.

Omigosh. Made it twice in as many months and there will be more to come as long as I can get a great heirloom tomato. When I add arugula in a sandwich, I kind of ball it up and smush it down. Stops it from being so unruly. I didn't toss it with the dressing first, just drizzled it on top. Made 2 gigantic sandwiches. Didn't find it too oily. Just too delicious.

My husband declared “eat your heart out, Panera!” Great way to celebrate the start of peak tomato season. Unlike most NYT Cooking recipes, took way LESS than the listed time- only about 30 minutes. Also calculate your own calories, if that’s your thing- for some reason the estimate is for 18 servings. (I used much less halloumi and oil/mayo than prescribed, and my sandwich turned out around 560 calories.)

this was delicious, and took closer to 30 minutes! I toasted the bread in the toaster and cooked the haloumi on the top rack of my oven under the broiler rather than in the pan. I find that this method browns the halloumi more easily without making it gummy. brush with olive oil first. I used butter lettuce instead of arugula, and did dress it, but made a dressing that was more lemon juice than olive oil. (mostly because I was running low) the sandwich wasn't at all overly greasy.
Add bacon to sandwich

Given the amount of prep to make this sandwich, I was underwhelmed with the result.  For my liking there was just too much lemon, too much mayo and not enough tomato. It's also a very messy sandwich to eat. We decided that we love the ingredients, but would rather have them all in a salad, cubing the halloumi, and adding a really good homemade crouton to replace the sandwich bread.
Has anyone tried this substituting feta or some other cheese for halloumi?
This was delicious!! I couldn’t find halloumi at my grocery store, so I breaded and fried up some feta, worked perfectly with the flavors!
Make Labneh and use that instead of fattening mayo for the spread. I've been eating this forever, very Middle Eastern
Assembled this as a salad with sourdough croutons.

As noted above tomato sandwiches are amazing. Might I suggest Duke’s mayo specifically?  Salt and pepper to taste on toasted bread. Only tomatoes that haven’t been refrigerated, as well as, only those that haven’t had the taste bred out of them.

Quick and simple.  Made it for 1. I used just a few thin slices of tomatoes and a few slices of halloumi on quinoa bread.  Just a touch of mayo on the outside for toasting of bread and the flavoured mayo inside (too taste).  A handful of lightly dressed arugula from the garden.  Really nice flavours.  Looking forward to making it with my garden tomatoes when they are ready!

I made this tonight for supper and it was amazingly good, even with the second-rate GF bread I am reduced to (which fell apart half way thru). I loved all the flavors and textures, and finding a new way to use halloumi. Highly recommend!

We enjoyed this sandwich very much. I dressed both the onions and greens in the Greek dressing (to which I added a touch more lemon juice and some garlic powder). Didn't make the mayo mix, but instead toasted the bread in a lightly oiled cast ion pan. Spread one slice of the sandwich with some, but not a lot. of mayo and sprinkled on coarsely ground black pepper. Will definitely make again.
Great sandwich. Since my wife doesn’t like arugula or raw onions, I skipped the arugula and marinaded the red onions in the Greek dressing - perfect! Moving this one to our high rotation recipes!
I forgot to buy tomatoes, and it was still delicious!!  Definitely a keeper!
Arugula != lettuce
Made this yesterday for lunch. Messy but delicious.  The halloumi took some time to brown on medium high heat, not the 2-3 minutes suggested. More like ten minutes.

This is really good! Made it as written except subbed fresh dill for oregano in the dressing since I had some to use (and I think it was even better), and romaine for arugula (what I had). Very low effort but tasty payoff. This is a good weeknight dish- definitely going into the rotation!

This is a fantastic sandwich. It’s an elaborate sandwich - sure, you could just throw things on some bread - but you could also zhuzh it up and you wouldn’t regret it. Not sure I ever followed a sandwich recipe before but I don’t regret it. I felt like we were eating at a fun and fancy sandwich shop instead of eating a cheese and tomato sandwich at home. I followed the recipe exactly and I wouldn’t change a thing. I served it with Charlie Bird’s Farro Salad which was a great compliment.
I never leave public comments, but I think this was the best sandwich I’ve ever made/eaten. Truly impeccable. I subbed vegan mayo throughout and it worked beautifully.

I'm not a mayo fan so I spread toasted sourdough with spicy labneh topped with sliced tomatoes, halloumi, and the dressed arugula (used the leftover lemon/garlic dressing from a favorite salad https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015707-lemon-garlic-kale-salad?unlocked_article_code=1.2k0.nCr2.sJmZ6RrHKJLb&smid=). I forgot the onion but the whole thing took 7 minutes and it was really very delicious. I am trying to think how I can package this for lunch tomorrow and have it stay crisp...

Wow, was this fabulous! We are putting this on the regular rotation. I made one tweak, out of frugality; I had 1/2c oil marinade leftover from artichokes, so I used some of that instead of the olive oil. Worked well. Not usually a fan of arugula, but it worked perfectly here, cutting some of the richness from the cheese and mayo. It took only 25 minutes, start to finish.
This sandwich was absolutely delicious! The garlic pepper mayo and the punchy arugula really make the whole sandwich. Will definitely be making this again!
Even though I forgot the onions, this was delicious. I cooked the halloumi in the morning to avoid turning on the stove later in the day, and it was a perfect hot weather vegetarian meal.

I never thought I would ever want children, but if I could with this sandwich- I would. It’s amazing.

The recipe forgets to add black pepper to the mayo ingredients, be sure to add it. I also added fresh garlic to the mayo spread as well as the vinaigrette for the arugula. But, I’m a garlic girl. Shake off extra dressing before putting on sandwich, as mine was a little juicy. 

This babe will go into permanent rotation!!!
This was...fine. Way too lemony, nothing to counterbalance all the acid. So many steps, components for something that was meh. My kitchen is trashed and for what? Will never make again.
Be sure to follow the instructions to toast the bread until golden in the pan. Otherwise it has a soggy texture. Learned that the hard way! Otherwise very tasty.
Fantastic! I did not have onion or arugula. To save calories I ate open faced. Still delicious!!!!
This is so amazing and delicious, but very, very greasy. I am going to try it again without grilling the bread with mayo on the outside. Toasting the bread seems like it would be crunchy and lighter.
White distilled vinegar or white wine vinegar?

Cut recipe in half for two so cut all ingredients in half as well. I was cautious bon lemon due to remarks from one viewer. I think it would have been fine as long as you are not heavy handed putting lemon on the halloumi. It’s very filling! 
As for someone’s remarks about trashing the kitchen… advice from years of experience…clean up as you go… put ingredients away as you use them and a sink of soupy water to wash as you use items.. no big deal.
This was really tasty. Howver, with how salty halloumi is on its own, I didn't add salt to anything else. Will definitely make again.

Sooooo oily! 
If I make this again, I would toast the bread in the toaster, add only a thin layer of mayo with lemon juice on the inside of the sandwich, and no dressing for the arugula. I couldn't even taste the halloumi under all of that mayo and oil.

I like the idea of this because I love halloumi, but when it’s sandwiched between bread, it really loses a lot of its appeal and its flavor. This dish would be much better as a dressed arugula salad with pan fried halloumi and tomatoes.
Delicious. I recommend reducing the mayo content and only using the barest layer when toasting the bread (or possibly none at all), to keep things getting too oily.
Perfect as written. Can't wait to make this again.

This is a good sandwich and very filling. We used one block of cheese and four slices of sourdough for four sandwiches and it was plenty. The cheese is very salty, so no extra salt is needed. Avocado would be a good addition, and the pepper mayo and salad dressing are fine, but not necessary if you’re in a hurry.
One of my favorite sandwiches ever! I actually like it better with the arugula dressed with just lemon juice and salt and pepper, and I don’t toast with mayo, I just toast in the toaster.
Grilled halloumi first and used leftover oil to toast the bread. Soaked up excess oil before adding lemon. Lightly dressed the sorrel with just a small part of the dressing.

This is the best sandwich I've had in years, I think. The only changes to the recipe were (1) I could not find halloumi, so I substituted that for bread cheese, and (2) I used grated garlic instead of garlic powder. Great recipe!

Delicious!
I went toasted with Guldens spicy brown, halloumi, heirloom tomato slice, sweet onion, arugula, halloumi, spicy brown, toast.

Pretty good and simple for a weeknight dinner. I mostly followed the recipe except that I toasted the bread and I grilled the halloumi…no sauté pan to clean. DH liked it and so I’ll probably make it again. 
FWIW I used only as much dressing and garlic mayo as I wanted.
The garlic mayo is delicious. We skipped the salad dressing and just piled on the arugula. Also just toasted the bread, didn’t fry in more mayonnaise. It’s a delicious sandwich.

This is a great flavor combination. I put the pickles right on the sandwich. With all of the juicy tomatoes, dressing, and lemon juice, the sandwich gets soggy quickly, so I want to try this with a thicker bread, like ciabatta.

We made this delicious sandwich last night and agree with previous comments that the arugula would be perfectly fine without the dressing. At the last minute we had to substitute the sourdough for seedless rye (discovered a tear in the wrapper of newly purchased sourdough- ugh) and will try it again with sourdough.

MARVELLOUS! I don’t like sandwiches but I just made this for myself and my boyfriend and we made FOUR EACH IN TOTAL !!! Every ingredient works to perfection together. Make sure to have a sturdy bread (no holes). Red onion elevates this exceptionally. Pair with a dry white wine!
@nancy you each ate four sandwiches in a sitting?
Absolutely incredible…finger licking good

This was an excellent sandwich!  We used purchased garlic mayo and our favorite Greek dressing we order from PA (Gazebo Room).  Only issue was with the bread falling apart so will look for a more sturdy sourdough next time.
It seems to me a panini press would simplify the preparation of this sandwich—has anyone tried using one?

Maybe I'm being overly optimistic, but this doesn't look like it takes almost an hour to make. Am I missing something?  I added up approximate times and it looks like 30 - 40 minutes at most. I guess I'll know more when I make it (which I'm planning to do).

Took the oily comments to heart:
- Toasted the bread without mayo
- Very lightly oiled the Arugula
- Was sparing with oil for the cheese.

 Honestly I wish I had dabbed the fried halloumi slices with a paper towel before applying lemon. Either way, rave reviews from the man.

Also, the halloumi was super expensive, so I bought some bread cheese to try (Juusto, I believe.) Just as good, and a little more sturdy that halloumi. And like, $5-6 cheaper.

If you have an ALDIs nearby, look in the cheese section for pouches of Cheese Fries Halloumi -Style Cheese Sticks. The cheese is cut in thickish strips that you can pan fry. I often cut it into croutons. MUCH cheaper than imported Halloumi for a pretty good product

My wife and I loved this! Especially my wife—the mix of salty and tangy has her number. Agree that the whole thing is a little greasy. While this didn’t interfere with our good time, I’d consider either dry-toasting the bread or grilling it with just a bit of olive oil. I added avocado because we had one and it fit right in. Also opted for open-faced.

So good! swapped the mayo for quark (mixed with garlic powder, lemon juice, a little bit of olive, kashmiri & italian chili powders), and did a quick pickle of shallots. Don't have patience to watch halloumi in pan so baked it in oven & then added in thick seeded bread to toast

The NYT Cooking Nutritional Analysis indicates that these 4 sandwich account for 18 servings which suggests that each sandwich serving would be cut into about 4 servings per person.  

It also suggests that the nutritional information should be multiplied by 4 for each ingredient such as sodium which would provide close to 3,000mg of sodium per sandwich.  Seems excessive.
I loved this sandwich! I didn’t put dressing on the arugula, but I did use the mayo as directed.  It was delicious.

This sandwich is delicious. A little confused by the one hour cook time considering it took less than 15 minutes to throw it together. I made it for one so didn’t measure/made some adjustments. 

Skipped seasoning the mayo, just seasoned the tomatoes and arugula heavily. Didn’t want to cut a lemon for a tiny squeeze so used apple cider vinegar instead and just a drizzle of olive oil for the arugula. Added avocado to be extra decadent. As many have said, layer order is key to any sandwich. 

Great use of halloumi. Yum yum yum.

Agreed with other comments that it was quite greasy. I don’t think the arugula needs dressing. I grilled the halloumi which is def the way to go with halloumi. It was delicious and VERY filling. I could do with half a sandwich. Served on my husband’s homemade sourdough and it was fantastic. Would make again but with less oil and split with the hubs.
Can I do this with paneer?

It was tasty! Wouldn’t toast with mayo and just regular toast next time. Didn’t make the dressing just used plain arugula and used some pickled red onions I had in the fridge. Really good! Also had to use bread cheese because no haloumi at the store. Still delicious! Kept its shape
One of the best sandwiches I’ve ever made. So simple, bright and flavorful
Very hard to find Halloumi where I live, so I use paneer, either store bought (Indian groceries) or homemade, which is also far less salty. The rest of the sandwich is unbeatable.

a banger
swapped the dressing for samin's house dressing cuz its what I had on hand

Made this as written, and next time I will probably just toast the bread in the toaster like I do when we make BLTs. It was highly flavorful, but a touch oily, and I would be willing to sacrifice that and remove a step or two from this yummy (but eight step) recipe.
While this was pretty good, I found that the lemon overpowered the taste of the halloumi and the arugula. I will try without the vinaigrette next time.
Goodness. Pure goodness!

I’ve made this as written and it was good but it took way too long for a sandwich or even a weeknight meal.  I made it again lightly toasting the sourdough in a toaster oven, no mayo, dressing the arugula with olive oil and lemon juice, putting plain kewpie on the bread when I assembled it.  Equally as delicious and half the time.

I made this twice, once as written.  The second time I toasted the bread in the toaster oven, used plain kewpie mayo, Roma tomatoes and quickly dressed the arugula with lemon juice and olive oil.  Equally as delicious and took much less time and fuss.
Use thin slices of bread.

This was insanely good. I made it as written except I didn't dress the arugula because (1) I'm lazy and (2) I'm not a huge fan of salad dressing anyway. But it was excellent without. Like jaw-droppingly good. I'm excited to make this in the summertime with some tomatoes fresh from the farmers market. Yummmm
This was a delicious sandwich! Followed the recipe exactly.

I ham and egged this recipe using Oacaxa cheese with dried oregano, crushed red pepper, and corn tortillas.  I substituted lemon for the lime and white mushrooms. The outcome was outstanding. I added a small garden salad, and a cold beer for a delicious dinner.  Since I have the taste of a peasant, I did not miss the fresh epazote.  A+ for the NY Times with this one.
There is NO substitute for fresh epazote. Like basil, its unique character is utterly lost when dried.
How much dried epazote would you use?
None. Dried epazote bears no resemblance whatsoever to fresh epazote. If you don’t have fresh epazote leave it out, or maybe substitute a sprinkle of Mexican oregano.
At what temperature do you bake the cheese? Is the oven preheated?
350 - Step 1.
350F, 20 minutes.  Yes, preheated
You can count on ovens always being preheated unless specifically noted otherwise.
Does medium pan equal 8 or 10 inch?

Please let’s not have people add that it’s not the way grandmother made it, or not really Mexican… :) 

Looks fab!
Pregnant women should not eat epazote. It can be cause contractions and miscarriage!
Huge hit and easy to make. Also hugely unhealthy, but absolutely worth it for special occasions!! Delicious.
Too much oil for the mushroom marinade.  I will still take Ninfa’s original recipe.
Epic fail.  I went and found Houston’s Mama Ninfa’s original recipe.  Queso A La Parrilla.  Less work, delicious.
Any substitute for caramel sauce?
You could try substituting corn syrup, honey, or molasses for the caramel sauce, although any of these would change the flavor of the dessert significantly.
If you're more specific about what you're substituting -- flavor? allergy? texture? -- you'll get a better answer.

My family baked a similar bar cookie that we called ‘Chews’ or ‘Chinese Chews’. The ingredients included brown sugar, butter, eggs, slivered almonds and shredded coconut. The recipe is called Toffee Nut Bars in a 1950’s version of the Betty Crocker cookbook. I still bake them and they are delicious.
As other said, not enough liquid. The accompanying article mentions butter, so I assume that a stick of butter is left out of the ingredients. As written, yuck!
How about melting the sugar in two sticks of melted butter? Then mixing in the eggs after the butter/sugar mixture cools?

I followed the directions carefully, but I could not cut the Chew Bread into squares as the recipe was still gooey.  I thought allowing it to set out for awhile might help, but it did not do much, if anything.  My kids and their cousin ate it right out of the pan with spoons while watching a movie.  It is too sweet for me, but I have already had a request for more of it from the kids.

Made as written, 15 min cooking time is correct. Oil is for the pan. Adding butter as someone suggested in the comments would dramatically change the texture (and the meaning, keep in mind this was a treat with limited ingredients.) They are good, not too sweet. I may dust with a bit of powdered sugar after they cool.

Great recipe and so exciting to learn about a new food. After oiling the pan, I added a parchment sling. I added 1 tsp vanilla, and blanched the pecans because nuts can irritate my mouth. The batter is stiff like cookie dough and needs to be pressed into the pan. The end result has a chewy texture like brownies and tastes like pralines but not as sweet.

I’m not a baker, because I don’t do exact measurements or even exact ingredients. Still, this recipe turned out great! Fluffy texture just out of the oven, hardening a bit (enough to cut cleanly) after 5-10 minutes. Here are the substitutions I made: used a 9"x9" pan (what I had), used unsifted fine rice flour (because gluten free), used raw sugar (what I had), and used Torani caramel sauce from the coffee-flavorings area of my grocery store. Very tasty, easy, fast.
So yummy!!! I made this right away and it made my day.

After reading the recipe and then the comments I am more confused than not.
cook time 40 minutes or 15 minutes? 1 tbsp canola oil? Is that right?
I was born and raised in the south but never heard of this recipe until today.
I understand 15 minutes of baking time that are included in a total preparation (cooking) time of 40 minutes. That makes sense to me.

Sounds great, but---Anyone know of a caramel sauce safe for someone with a treenut and peanut allergy?
Will try pepitas instead of pecans.
Hershey's (depending on your allergy comfort levels.  We trust their labeling and manufacturing)

I just made this chew bread recipe tonight as written. Perfect even though it was difficult to spread before baking. It came out really nice. Cooled it for 5 min and cut into bars. It’s not sweet but there’s a wholesomeness to it. Would be great with a little caramel drizzle and some whip if you’d want it as a proper sweet after dinner. Nice to take for a picnic or hike as a treat.
I made it and it is easy and so delicious, not too sweet. I made it with the ingredients and methods in the Times recipe. I think I'll eat one now.
Made these tonight, taste fantastic but they came out more like a cookie than bread or brownie… any suggestions??
Bland and tasteless. Not very good. Regular blondies are much, much better.

The sauce this produces is basically what we call a pipian verde in Mexico. If you substitute chilies poblano for the bell peppers and use a comal or heavy fry pan for browning the tomatillos, onion, chilies, and garlic, you are making a simple pipian with this recipe and I like it very much. I substitute a good quality tahini for the roasted and ground sesame seeds and it works well.

This is outstanding. Made as is with just a few method changes to simplify and reduce pan usage. 
Season and brown the thighs in the Dutch oven (because why wouldn’t you?!?), remove them and immediately fry the garlic in the same pot. Deglaze with white wine and add the broiled veg and stock. Return the chicken with juices and off you go. 
Also, add the ground nut mixture to the blender to puree with the broth, veg, and cilantro. 
Comforting, colorful, delicious.

I haven't made this yet, but I'd certainly like to.  Unfortunately, I'm one of those with the bad cilantro gene.  What would my fellow cooks suggest...substituting an equal amount of flat-leaf parsley, substituting a different herb, or just eliminating the cilantro altogether and substituting nothing?
Me too...I will be following to see what people suggest.

Maybe a little parsley for the green and a squeeze of lemon for the freshness, although the tomatillos are a bit tart. Nothing else tastes like cliantro. Do you have a problem with corriander? You might try a pinch of that.

Hey Bill,
My vote is just to leave off the cilantro. It's possible that a spritz of fresh lime juice might be nice just before serving, but any other herb would probably take this recipe in the wrong direction.

I make a similar dish frequently, it's great! If you are toasting the pumpkin seeds (and you should), then start with raw pumpkin seeds, not roasted. Take out the chicken after 15-20 minutes or you have tasteless chewy chicken.

Made this tonight. The only change I made was to follow Dan’s tip to remove the chicken after 20 minutes, which I’ll definitely repeat in the future. This is a bit labor intensive but worth it for a Sunday dinner. I love the combination of peppers, tomatillos, jalapeño and cilantro. I don’t think subbing or omitting the cilantro would work. It’s a big part of this dish. Perhaps try a recipe for pepian instead.
This is a perfect post thanksgiving dish, made with leftover turkey and homemade stock!

Cooked as per the recipe and after adding the roasted pumpkin and sesame seeds I was worried that the flavor became overwhelmingly pumpkin. Give it time to all work together because in the end it was perfect. Note to self, don't seed the jalapeno. In the end I had no heat in the dish and added 1/4 teaspoon of red pepper flakes to give it some heat.
The whole family loved this soup!!
I can’t wait to do this.
When does the 20 minutes of prep start? After the mise en place is ready? This is delicious, but I’ve been actively working for nearly two hours!
I believe cilantro was known to the ancient Egyptians and is native to Asia and Europe. If the Mayans used it it must have been brought there by the Spanish.

@Name Padma The Maya K’iche people are still alive and in large numbers in Guatemala. They are still adapting all kinds of recipes and adding ingredients to their culinary tradition. This is definitely a post-columbian dish but one that has its origins in their traditional cooking methods.
Those who don't like fresh cilantro and want the green could use parsley.. which I find more or less flavor neutral.

I made this tonight. It's very hearty with the fat from seeds, chicken, olive oil. I love the basic flavor but it could use some brightening, maybe with lime juice, or coriander seeds. It absorbed a surprising amount of salt with the potatoes, carrots, seeds. 

Great trick to thicken with ground seeds and pureed veggies. Somehow it didn't stick to the bottom and risk scorching at all!

Really interesting recipe if not a little bland. I added some cumin for warmth and dried chipotle for a little heat. I would make this again, but with poblano or hatch chilis. Maybe sub polenta for rice? Overall quite good, however.

Just made this last night. It requires a fresh squeeze of lime. Next time I will use a mortar and pestle to mash up the sesame seeds before combining with the pumpkin because my food processor couldn’t get a good enough grind on the combo.
Us Guatemalans are often accused of adding lime juice to everything, so no surprise here.

Very delicious!  I made a 2/3rds batch and it was a lot.  My tip: Grind up the seeds in a spice grinder (mine is AKA a coffee grinder LOL) in batches.  Worked great!  Stop before you make nut butter though.
Pulled chicken after 30 min. Needed salt. Labor intensive and $$ but delicious. Maybe grind sesame seeds a bit more

I never use green bell peppers. I never have. I tried them once; they are just awful, irredeemable.  Green peppers are best used for animal feed. I guess I’d leave bell peppers out or … use red bell peppers — either raw or roasted.

This soup was pretty good, but I feel it didn’t have as much flavor as I was expecting.   If I made it again, I would use poblano peppers instead of flavorless green bell peppers.  I also would blend the seed mixture more (fine grain of sand) as I didn’t love the texture of the grainy seeds in the finished product

Fantastic recipe, really delicious. My only advice is that you make it with another person so the two of you can share the labor. There are a lot of steps, none of them hard, but time-consuming. It’s so worth the time, though!! This is a truly special dish with complex flavors.
Really good, using yucca instead of potatoes works well

Yum! Different than anything I have ever cooked. Made this during a 10* winter snow storm day. The flavor in this recipe comes from good quality ingredients- don’t skimp. It’s hearty, but light. Healthy, but doesn’t leave you feeling like a rabbit. It is a warm, full flavor. Not a knock your socks off, full bodied, bold style.

This is so good. My kids (6 and 9) happily eat it. There are definitely easier ways to use less dishes and, in my experience, it doesn’t change the outcome. Also the chicken doesn’t need that much time cooking unless you like eating rubber. This gets made at least once a month in our house.
What suggestion have worked for you in reducing the number of dishes?
Dumb question: when I went shopping for ingredients, I found both roasted pumpkin seed kernels and whole roasted pumpkin seeds. I went with the kernels, but should I jave used the whole seeds?

It’s great but for low-carb, substitute Jerusalem artichokes for the potatoes. Also, why not buy bone-in thighs with skin? Use the skin to make cracklings, and just remove the bone when shredding the cooked chicken. Cheaper and far more flavor.

Try culantro or celery leaves as a favor substitute. Parsley would brighten the color as would the onkin srems.
I never add carros or potatoes, nor the seeds.
Bill, same issue re cilantro in my household. Best substitute we've found is culantro aka recao, chadon beni (pronounced shadow benny) and Mexican coriander.
Basil would be a good substitute for cilantro instead of parsley.

This is somewhat similar to one version of the Southwestern favorite posole, subbing potatoes for posole's hominy and adding the pumpkin and sesame seeds. Basically, a chicken-and-tomatillo soup. 

Juice from a lime makes a good complement to the chicken broth.

Skip the cilantro if you have to. The flavor is really a blend of the other ingredients. 

I'm not a fan of the sesame seeds. So I work with the green pumpkin seeds. Adds creamy depth without making the broth a pale green.
Would like to use canned tomatillos because of limited availability of fresh ( time).    Would this work?

No bell peppers will be prepared or consumed in relation to this recipe! Never had them served in Guatemala and some of us can’t eat them . Lots of other kinds of peppers but not Bell. So what would your substitution suggestion be? Chapines represent!  
No point in a sub for cilantro though. Leave it off.
Boil an entire chicken first and use that broth as the base. Then quarter it and finish the recipe. Makes the taste even deeper.
Are the pumpkin seeds supposed to be hulled or unhulled?

This looks delicious! but green bell pepper is the only pepper that violently upsets my stomach. Which would be better - substituting yellow bell peppers (which would change the color - it might look muddy and unappetizing) or substituting a mild green pepper such as poblanos (which would keep the color but change the flavor)?

I really wanted to like this dish, but it was just ok -- came out bland, even with some extra seasoning that I added (toasted cumin seeds along w/ the sesame and pumpkin). I think the potatoes contributed to that. I might make it again with fewer potatoes and more carrots. Also would try to spice it up a bit more -- maybe add a hotter pepper like a serrano. Added a squeeze of lime juice at the end as others suggested and that did pep it up a little.
Hi, cliantro is coriander. It's the same plant/leaf.
Substitute cauliflower instead of potatoes
What's Mayan is Mayan and what's yours is Mayan!

Made this tonight using poblanos instead of bell peppers and serranos rather than jalapeños. Toasted the pepitas and sesame seeds. Blistered the onion and chiles on a comal because it's easier. I tasted along the way and adjusted the salt. I can't imagine this without cilantro! 
Next I'm going to stick it in the fridge and eat it tomorrow because stew always tastes better the next day
There are so few Guatemalan recipes available for home cooks.  Thanks SO much!  I can't wait to try it.

This took well more than two hours but was DELICIOUS. I would suggest a stopping point after step 5: you can blender the liquid and freeze in batch, and then cut down your subsequent dinner prep time.

This was a LOT of work. We really enjoyed the end product but I’m not sure the balance of effort to reward was quite worth it. Luckily it made a ton so we have frozen leftovers for another day. Excellent experiment but way too much work to put in rotation.

This was excellent! Time consuming but a lot of it can be prepped ahead. Made for a family gathering of 5 (there was PLENTY leftover). I too browned the chicken first, removed it, then added it back in with the potato step. Used two whole serranos with seeds and still could have used a little more heat.
I took out the chicken after 20 minutes as suggested and used 2 jalapeños. Couldn’t find unsalted pumpkin seeds so eliminated the salt with the garlic. Just perfect. Will make again.

This dish was amazing!  I did reduce the amount of chicken broth and the seed mixture by half but used the recommended amounts of everything else as I wanted it to be more stew-like. I did not the seed the jalapeño, used 1 poblano and 2 green peppers. I might up the heat on the peppers (maybe a Serrano, or more poblanos as someone else suggested). Served it with a side salad- sliced avocado on lettuce with a relish of tomato, red onion, cilantro and lime juice. Wonderful!
Any recommendations to adjust this for an electric pressure cooker?

This is delicious! I didn't have all the "proper" ingredients: used roasted, salted pumpkin seeds, canned tomatillos (which I drained and added directly to the broth), only 1 3/4 lb chicken thighs and five small redskin potatoes which turned out to be plenty. I bought some poblano peppers knowing they would add more flavor. Otherwise followed the recipe. The roasted, ground seeds added a great note. A little bit of work but worth it.

Followed recipe except I picked up already cooked and chopped chicken from deli and used new potatoes. Will make this again in winter as it doesn't need seasonal vegetable. Excellent and different from tortilla soup which is another stand by.
Depth of flavor was wonderful.

This was very good, but I felt like it needed a little something, so I squeezed a little lime juice on it and that did the trick. Toasting the seeds and charring the vegetables is definitely worth the effort. The recipe does make a lot of food. I ate the leftovers for a week.

I made it and it was delicious and lit up our mouths. My 2 year old was licking her bowl :) I did substitute the jalapeño with a large-ish Serrano pepper, with seeds, whose taste I prefer in this kind of dish. I was having a brain blank and made it with pumpkin seeds (like it says in recipe), shell on, as opposed to pepitas which make way more sense haha - it was still super yum but I can’t wait to try with pepitas.

Very tasty, which I earned over the hours of making this stew. It’s a pain, to be honest. I won’t make it again unless I have time to break down the steps—maybe do the broiling and seed toasting and grinding the night before, make the stew up to pureeing in the morning (so it can cool before pureeing,) then reheating and serving that evening. The one upside is that we have stew to eat for the next couple of nights.

Culantro is a great substitute for cilantro. Harder to find, but the outcome of the taste is the same. We use it as a work-around for a coriander allergy. Not sure if it'll work for folks who think cilantro tastes like soap but it's worth a try. Most good "farmers" markets have it. We find it at a big Asian market near our house that has a great variety of fresh herbs.

I made this last night and I agree with another contributor that it’s a bit labor intensive.  We loved the smoky undertones of the roasted vegetables.  Next time I would substitute a poblano possibly for one of the green peppers.  The heat from the jalapeno did not overwhelm rest of the sauce.  It was worth all the work.

I am glad I made this recipe but it was a lot of work.  Two of us tag-teamed this recipe and it took us over 2.5 hours of constant work, but we had fun.  We fed this to 12 people, including 6 kids, and everyone liked it and a couple people loved it, but I still feel like the recipe isn't quite there.  As if there is one spice missing that would push it over the top, but I don't know what it is missing.  We tried lime juice and maybe that helped. 6.5 cups of stock yielded a very thick stew.

Delicious and a little out of the ordinary thanks to the seeds. It's an involved recipe that takes a lot of time but I thought it was a perfect Sunday night dinner. I followed Mike D. and Nate's suggestions. Also next time I won't take the seeds out of the jalapeño as someone suggested. I cut the cilantro in half but provided fresh cilantro on the side as well as  lime wedges. Also it did require some added salt.

My husband and I made this tonight. It is pretty labor intensive, but it is super delicious so worth it to us. I halved the salt and jalapeño, but next time I won’t do that because ours needed a little more salt and didn’t have any spice to it.
Next time we may make a double batch so we can freeze some and it’ll be worth the long prep.

I would definitely recommend just cutting slices of the chicken rather than tearing it up after boiling it.

Adds more varied texture.

Add it raw just after adding the potatoes and carrots.

Also, serve with tortilla chips for even MORE variety in texture.

Delicious recipe overall.

Phenomenal!! I hate to be one of those people that made a bunch of changes however: used poblanos instead of green peppers, tahini instead of sesame seeds, and rotisserie chicken. I used the chicken carcass to cook with the roasted veggies and then add it back in toward the end.

So good! The pumpkin seeds are such a great addition.

Terrific! Very versatile and easily adjusted if required. I realized (to late) that the pepper I used was a "mystery pepper" from my garden, which literally set my whole face (and the dish) on fire. Added some ginger, coconut milk and fresh orange juice and toned everything down beautifully, with a nice hint of sweet. Added some creme fraiche at the table for anyone who wanted it less spicy. Absolutely lovely dish! Will make this again!

Kind of screwed up by adding an extra onion and skipped that extra step with the garlic completely and just added it with the chicken and tomatillo, but still turned out pretty good. Cooked the chicken for the full 35 minutes it says and it turned out fine and had that pull-apart texture. Does need some work on the flavor. Will admit that I kind of under salted it, but added a second jalapeno and it still didn't have as much heat or acid as I thought.

Delicious as written! I doubled the tomatillos and increased the amount of chicken to make it heartier. Did not disappoint, made eight servings. Perfect for the cold weather we have. Wish I had used the turkey in the deep freeze like another poster suggested. Deseeded the jalapeños, and there was very little heat, but that’s OK because our baby eats what we eat and it was perfect for her.
My husband liked it.  I thought it was super blah.  He has a lot of left overs to eat.
This was an amazing dish. I scaled it down to 2/3, but used a full jalapeno, so maybe it was a bit more intense, but it hit just right.
Has anyone successfully adapted this recipe for slow cooker instead of stove top?

The flavor of this stew is great, but be very careful to fully pulverize the seeds - if there are any larger bits they make the texture quite unpleasant. Also don’t be afraid to be aggressive with the salt - especially if using an unsalted broth.

Had an abundance of tomatillos and green peppers (not my favorite) from our CSA and stumbled upon this recipe. Followed it exactly. Just the right amount of heat. Perfectly salted and flavorful. The ground seeds help thicken it up and gives it a nice texture. This recipe is not as labor intensive as others made it out to be. Will definitely make again.
My family and I really enjoyed this. Because there is a good amount of potato, we skipped the rice the second time we made it.

Delicious! Would definitely make again. I roasted a whole chicken and added it to the pot for maybe 20 min to soften and took it out to cool and remove the meat from the bones. The grocery store was out of jalapeños so it wasn't spicy, but still amazing flavor!
The recipe doesn't mention taking the charred skins off the veg. Should I, or does the char add to the flavor?
@Ann H  keep the charred skins on

This recipe looked delicious and I wanted to cook it immediately but didn’t have all the ingredients so I improvised with what I had. I was cooking for 2 so made half a recipe. I used fajita chicken, which I browned with the onions and a little cumin and cinnamon. I didn’t have jalapeños or green peppers so I subbed a can of hatch chilis, and didn’t have tomatillos but had a can of roasted diced green tomatoes, so I added the tomatoes and chilis after slightly cooking the fajita strips. I only added 1 cup of broth which seemed like enough. The cilantro and seed mixture really added something special! I didn’t blend the soup, just ate it as a stew. It was delicious, but I look forward to making it as written.

OUT OF THIS WORLD!! 
Truly one of the best things I’ve ever cooked and  eaten. And I’ve been cooking and creating lol! for 50 years. 
Did not change one thing!! 
Followed instructions exactly! 
Only added a little more chicken because my husband is a meat eater.  
Hats off and many thanks to his grandmother!! 
Would have loved to come up in her kitchen. 
But my mother was a fabulous cook too!!

title: Suspiro a la Limeña (Caramel Pudding With Port Meringue) Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: ....and the egg discussion - geez.  It's clear - 8 yolks. 2 whites.

I find that mixing the milks takes more like 50 minutes. Also every time I try to add the port I get a brown soupy meringue and have a very hard time getting it fluffy. When I’ve just done sugar and egg white and skipped the port I get a passable merengue and the dish still passes snuff for my husband-this is his favorite dessert.
Are you using 2 egg whites, or 10? (The recipe is not clear. See other comments.)

Sounds to me like 8 eggs total, separated.
Use 8 yolks plus 2 whites for the custard.
Use remaining 6 whites for the meringue.  

The port wine will need to be evaporated to a thick syrup or else the meringue will be runny when it's combined.
Could the milks be mixed using a sous vide machine?

I've never seen a recipe that tells you to determine when something is whisked enough by measuring the temperature of the inside base of the mixing bowl. There must be another way to tell. Peaks? Stiffness?
It's essentially an Italian meringue, and, as in this recipe, I was taught to beat the syrup into the egg whites until the bowl was cool to the touch.
Does it use 8 or 10 egg whites--I don't understand the instructions.

I just read the recipe. It sounds like you use 8 yolks and 2 whites. To me, it is either 2 whites or 10 whites. If the latter, it should say" 8 eggs, separated, plus 2 additional egg whites". I should think that a cup of port would be more likely distributed between 10 egg whites, judging by the color of the meringue in the photo.

It seems to me that ten egg whites would make lots more meringue than needed for 6-8 desserts.
Perhaps using the port syrup as a thin layer over or under the custard would be a lovely visual pop and bright note while retaining the flavor of the recipe.

First of all, the recipe is clear as day - 8 yolks and 2 egg whites.

You can confirm this with some general knowledge of cookery. 8 or 10 whites would make an insane amount of meringue. And egg whites have no place in a custard. 

Furthermore, the egg whites are not raw - they are turned into an Italian meringue with the addition of the hot syrup.
I think a little lemon juice added to the custard would balance the richness of this recipe.
Let us know after you have tried it. Also be sure to let us know how it affects the flavor of the meringue with the ruby port.
Just for the record, uncooked eggs are not safe.  Ms Morales should have mentioned ths. It's true, the risk is low, but why take any risk when there are so many safe recipes.
Just for the record, you can buy pasteurized eggs.
The hot milk mixture will cook the eggs.
@Daniel Brownthe egg yolks will cook with the milks.  The egg white will cook with the hot syrup.
I believe this recipe calls for a total of eight eggs separated with two of the whites retained for the merangue.
The directions are perfectly clear. Use 8 egg yolks for the custard, use 2 of egg whites for the meringue leaving 6 egg whites for another use: ie pavlova, egg white omelette etc etc
Made this exactly as directed. It took 50 mins to even start thickening on the stove. After refrigerating, it did not set.
Being Peruvian, i can tell you that this dessert is maybe the most popular and beloved one among us. We usually use 4 eggs ( 4 yolks for the custard and 4 for the merengue)
For the egg whites, can I use the whites from two of the eggs that I'm cracking for the yolks?
@Andy of course!

"8 large egg yolks" means use EIGHT large egg yolks.
"2 egg whites" means use TWO egg whites. Not 8. Not 10. TWO.
What is Red Port? Will any port work??
@Princekorv any port will work. As long as it’s red. (ie not a white port.)
The recipe is crystal clear.  Eight yolks plus two whites.
Reading through all the understandable confusion around the number of egg yolks and whites, I decided to try a different recipe. Here you go! https://www.eatperu.com/suspiro-limeno-recipe/

@CLM. 
WOW! This entire recipe uses TWO EGGS for the entire recipe, with exactly the same volume of milks, vanilla, and port. Six portions instead of 6 to 8. I feel compelled to try this one first.

Interesting the confusion about the amount of eggs!. The directions are clear but some readers need the added clarifying step. Separate 8 eggs. Use the yolks in the custard and reserve 2 egg whites for the merengue. Use the other 6 egg whites for a different purpose. The hot port syrup pasteurizes the merengue but I'm not sure how you'd feel the inside of the bowl!
Tasty enough. But even for someone with a serious sweet tooth, I don't think I'll make this again, it's pretty over the top.

Two of my guy’s favorite things are dulce de leche and port. So when I spotted this a few days ago and knew I had to make it. I didn’t have any trouble with the custard coming together, though I did cook the milks at a higher temperature than called for. Stirred with a rubber spatula near constantly but it reached the right consistency in about 30 minutes. Custard set up beautifully and the port meringue was lovely. Everything was delicious, though it is REALLY rich so could only eat a few bites. But it’s like eating spoonfuls of caramel. Super decadent.
Allergic to alcohol… any good substitutes?
Grape juice made into a syrup?
Given the amount of sugar and port - it must be 10. Should read 8 eggs separated plus 2 additional egg whites
@jeanne año, it is 8 egg yolks and use only 2 egg whites for the meringue.
I believe it's 8 egg yolks.  And it's 2 egg whites, not 10 egg whites.

Robert please reread the recipe.  Egg whites- stiff peaks.  For others who question the eggs- when you separate the 8 eggs for the custard, reserve 2 of the whites for the meringue.  I love the history of the name.  It seems like English never gets to be a romance language.  In the meantime I'll rename some tasty recipes with memories of past paramours.
Passion for Peaches (on my phone article Comments) suggested skipping the SCM - Sweetened Condensed Milk, and instead using Dulce de Leche and adding egg yolks.

I agree that the ingredients are not very clear when it comes to the eggs. The corn-meal traditional in most Central/South American regions is made with what we call "hominy," (corn kernels boiled with calcium hydroxide, washed and dried before hulling and grinding).  This treatment is called "nixtamalization," and prevents dietary diseases such as kwashiorkor and pellagra as well as neutralizing some fungal toxins.  

In Southern US - where pellagra was a serious problem into the 20th century - wheat flour was added to prevent the disease.

For our general knowledge, what happens if one uses regular cornmeal? Or stated differently, what are the cooking/baking characteristics of pre-cooked versus regular cornmeal? I will look this up, but it would be great explained here.

Great explanation of cornmeal here, including pre-cooked: https://www.bonappetit.com/story/how-to-buy-and-use-cornmeal I’m sure one can experiment with regular cornmeal in this recipe, although it may require additional liquid such as buttermilk.

If looking for a substitute, note that P.A.N. cornmeal has not been nixtamalized. When looking at other corn flours in a Latin foods section, I found that most were. Tortillas, tamales, hominy, and grits use nixtamalized corn. Apparently this process increases the nutritional value, but it also changes the flavor.

Lynn - The P.A.N. product uses a protease enzymatic nixtamalization process developed by Luis Caballero Mejias in the early 1950s.  Based on technques of the "corn wet-milling" industry, it is useful in a large industrial setting. Mejias' process reduces the energy, water, and time required to produce a given amount of "cooked" corn meal.  Added nutrients are a common feature of these large-scale capital-intense manufactures, as well as undesired byproducts and chemical residues.

JALAPEÑO CORN CASSEROLE
1    stick butter 
1    large onion-chopped
1-2 chopped jalapeños 
3    eggs
1    large can cream style corn 
2    large cans whole kernel corn & juice
2     boxes Jiffy Corn Bread Mix
2     cups grated cheddar cheese
Sauté onions & jalapeños in butter.
In bowl, mix beaten eggs, corn bread mix, creamed corn & whole kernel corn with its juice.
Lightly grease a 9 X13 in. pan. Pour corn bread mix into pan
Spoon onions & jalapeños & cheese on top. Bake 375 for 45 mins.Yum!

Venezuelan hallaca has earned its spot in my kitchen through a recipe encapsulating the true spirit of celebration. Featuring a seamless blend of corn dough and meticulously spiced stews enveloped in leaves, every mouthful becomes a sensory expedition into the heart of Venezuelan festivities. Brimming with genuine flavors and refined textures, this dish goes beyond broadening all flavor horizons; it acts as a cultural conduit, transporting me directly to Venezuela's festivals.

On this, I would also add that there are massive regional differences in how the Hallaca is made in Venezuela. Because it is so labour intensive you don't have time to experiment with the recipe. SO each family creates its own and tastes different. In Caracas we wouldn't put potatoes or carrots, for instance. In Margarita they put aubergine. It is really versatile.

This is almost identical to the Nicaraguan nacatamal, which is usually eaten on Sundays.
I think every tropical country (including those in the Eastern hemisphere) has some version of filling steamed in a banana leaf wrapper.

True, Cuba has its own version of tamales too, but certain ingredients are also not found in Cuban tamales, for example fried chicken or ketchup, and I would be disagreeing with those that insist that every family in every country has its own recipe, so everything is fair game.  Latin food is not just food, it reflects the culture of each country, even if there are subtle variations amongst local recipes.

The author of the first great reference book of Venezuelan cuisine, Don Armando Scannone, once said that "The tamale is a case of corn with a little filling. The hallaca is an excellent filling with a very fine case of corn". This perfectly defines what this emblematic Venezuelan dish consists of, not only gastronomically, but also culturally and societally. Its recipe is an excellent starting point for all the varieties that exist in the country.

When I suddenly found myself living in rural Pennsylvania as a young boy in the pre-Goya 1960’s having moved from Caracas what I missed most were Hallacas at Christmas and quanabana. 
This recipe is incorrect. A typical Hallaca does not have garbanzos, potatoes, and carrots and Is missing many of the sweet/salty combinations which make up the basis of Venezuelan cuisine. This hallaca is more of an Andean version typical of a Columbian pastele without the annato tinted masa.
