Okay, let’s be honest: this is not going to fool your abuela and why would you want to fool her anyway??? Tell her you have a delicious vegetarian dish that reminds you of her pernil (but of course not as good as hers!) and let her enjoy it in its own right! Because it’s delicious. And every member of my family inhaled it. And no, it isn’t pork. But it’s great.

Notes: i used the red goya sofrito because that’s what my grocery store had. It’s fine, homemade would be better. Made my own adobo.

Any tips for adapting if you have access to fresh jackfruit? 
Would it still need brining or would you want to boil it with some added salt to compensate?

It's likely not the answer you're looking for, but it's difficult to get recipes right when trying to substitute fresh jackfruit in a recipe that was only tested with canned. If you make this and want a similar result as tested my advice is to use the canned even though you have fresh jackfruit available.
I used fresh jackfruit that was cooked in a pressure cooker. No salt. Excellent results.

I just made this, with homemade sofrito and sazon, and used it as filling for tamales, and it is insanely delicious. Good enough to give as holiday gifts for my favorite humans. Thank you! The first time I trued a jackfruit recipe, it was awful, so you’ve redeemed this unusual plant for me.
Jackfruit seeds are edible. Why discard them?
We have a jackfruit tree, used pieces cooked in pressure cooker and drained.

I followed this recipe exactly except I doubled it and it was SO delicious!! I couldn’t find sofrito in the store so I made it homemade which was super easy to do. The flavor in this dish is amazing and the texture is so juicy. You could use this jackfruit so many ways. I plan to use the leftovers to make nachos. Seriously an amazing recipe.

Delicious dish, but I skip the added salt as the adobo and sazon are salt based. 

Absolutely worth the effort to use this to top nachos, or sprinkle on top of beans and rice.
Fresh jackfruit is not the same. The canned version is made with unripe jackfruit, use the canned one.
Delicious, using the sofrito recipe from NYT and store bought Sazon and Adobo. If you have a convection oven, you probably only need 20 minutes versus 30 minutes without convection.
Used a 14oz can of Jackfruit instead of 20 and it was still a okay. Took an extra few minutes in the oven and came out a little saltier.

Very nice. My jackfruit was "young" so there were very few seeds and they were soft, and the pieces broke into small bits. This ended up with lots of crispy stuff that we basically used as a topping for rice and beans. Will make again.
Made for Easter with coconut rice and pigeon peas (and the vegan pudding pie) - spectacular success. Flavor was great, not too spicy.
Can fresh Jackfruit be used instead? Easy to find nowadays
This is one of the best recipes I've ever made. Restaurant quality. Extraordinary flavor and texture.
Am I really the only one who found this to taste bland?
Where’s the garlic at though?

I am surprised that there is no mention of the insane amount of salt in this.  I don’t generally dial back what a recipe says about salt because I trust the process, but this just tasted like a a textured salt lick.  A huge disappointment and waste of time, money, and energy.

This sparks a wonderful memory. Back in the 80s my Haitian roommate introduced me to cremas. Somehow I tracked down a recipe and made it for my Tennessee family. It was a huge hit. I guess now is a good time to revive it.

I was looking for something “eggnog-ish” (creamy, sweet, thick and boozy) that was quick and easy to make. I followed the recipe except I used only 1 can of condensed milk and a whole can of coconut milk, and omitted lime zest because I didn’t have any (I will infuse and strain later when I get some). Anyhoo, I cannot imagine using 2 cans of sweetened condensed milk because holy moly, this is sweet! But it’s definitely a keeper. Can’t wait to add it to my coffee!
Love it! So delicious.
Made this for this Christmas Eve and everyone loved it. Much easier that eggnog and not as cloying. Used only one can of condensed milk since that is what I had and it was as sweet as we wanted.
Skip the coconut for crema de vie...so much better.
There is room in this world for more than one holiday drink.
This coquito without the coco lopez. Coquito is an egg free drink. Those adding eggs are copying eggnog.
Wondering if this will work with all evaporated milk (it's what I have on hand), in place of sweetened condensed, maybe adding extra sugar as needed.
I found my old recipe. It had milk and separated eggs in place of sweetened condensed milk. I suspect you'd need a thickening agent if you forgo the sweetened condensed milk
Hi! What is the purpose of the evaporated milk? What does it add to drink? Thanks!

The original recipe, as transcribed in 1993 by famed mixologist Calvin Broadus, calls for Seagram's Gin and an indo accompaniment, with each drinker providing his own cup and (hopefully) chipping in on the alcohol purchase.  A fancier version, often attributed to Andre Young, uses Tanqueray and is typically accompanied by the bubonic chronic.  Both recipes are delicious, but I find that the first produces a more contemplative, "laid back" effect, while the second poses greater challenges.
I thought I heard of other  versions of this drink but couldn’t remember the mixologist thanks for the reminder. You are correct the recipe from Dr Young is a more stronger version.
There are so many other flavors at play here that using Tanqueray seems like an unnecessary extravagance. I opted for a less expensive gin and it was just fine.

Lovely drink I have often enjoyed under a different name, the venerable Bronx cocktail.  This recipe is essentially the same as a Bronx.  Here is the Food Network recipe:

In a cocktail shaker, combine 1 ounce gin, 2 ounces orange juice and 1/2 ounce each sweet and dry vermouth. Shake with ice for 30 seconds, then strain into a cocktail glass; garnish with an orange twist.

Many decades ago when my girlfriend and I were below the legal drinking age in KY where laws were meant to be broken, ordering this drink in a restaurant made us feel very sophisticated.
Thanks for the memories.
A different kind of a summer cocktail. Will make again then. Bit in December.

Oddly I thought the white vermouth would add a little “salt” taste to it but I found it distracting. Next time I make it I used blood oranges freshly squeezed, and for the orange liquor I only had Grand Marnier, so used it, and omitted the white vermouth. To me it tasted perfect.  I do understand its use, increasing the drinks depth, along with the sweet vermouth. All in all a definite sipper in this summers backyard entertaining!
There is a difference between dry and white vermouth. Dolin has, for example Dolin Dry And Dolin Blanc. Martini and Rossi have a Dry and a Bianco.
I used my tortilla press to make the dough rounds.  Much faster than rolling them out.
Add the filling ingredients to the pot at the same time– if you attempt to melt the sugar and add the pumpkin too fast, it will crystallize and become incredible difficult to recombine.
Can these be deep fried? Thinking about frying a turkey, so these might be fun to throw in the hot oil after the bird comes out.
I  would fry them before cooking the bird. The turkey will give the oil a flavor you might not want in your empanadas.
Step 8 = Repeat this process with the other half of the dough, thereby yielding the 24 empanadas indicated at the top of the recipe? Or am I missing something?
I wish I'd seen this comment!  I made the recipe with 12 large empanadas -- not good, too doughy.
Sean, you might try a little chile of some type or maybe even a bit of black pepper or smoked paprika.
I used this recipe to make empanadas with a savory potato filling borrowed from Pakistani samosas. Awesome.
What if you use butter instead of shortening? How about Crisco instead of lard? I have lots of butter for pie crust but no shortening.
Any substitutes for the shortening? I haven't used any shortening in decades.
Lard. Leaf lard. I normally don't use shortening either, but sometimes it is a necessary evil. I always try to use leaf lard in place of shortening.  In my opinion it is a less "evil" fat.
Coconut oil is like the consistency of lard, and I use it often in baking.

Well, maybe you should start using shortening again because there are applications where it will produce a superior result over butter. In general, shortening / lard snobs are shortchanging themselves and those they cook for.
How long before eating can these be made? Thanksgiving Day is chaotic, so I’d like to have made these perhaps 1-2 days earlier.
Pumpkin puree is already very sweet, so are these spices. Doesn't seem to pack much punch, I'm curious how others improve on this setup.
Pumpkin purée is not sweet. Are you accidentally buying the canned pumpkin pie filling?
24 is a lot for just 2 of us. Can these be frozen before baking?
Any idea if it will work with a store-bought crust? Like Goya?
Can I prepare these and bake a day later?
Used shortening, per instructions. Dough wasn’t as tender as I might have liked, but overall good results and I’ll make again. The pumpkin filling was just right amount of sweetness.
I’d like to try a gluten free version. I read in some other empanada recipes to try using a store bought pie crust- I have premade gf pizza dough. Think that could work??

I followed the recipe, but the pastry wasn’t as good as I thought it would be. The filling is also underwhelming. 

Planning to add orange zest to the filling and chopped pecans to the next batch. Yes?

I used sweet potatoes instead of pumpkin, steamed in an instant pot. Modified the dough recipe to make it gluten free (used mixture of g-f flour substitute, g-f oat flour, g-f almond flour).  I used oil instead of solid shortening.  It was very good.

Don't be afraid of the lard.  It suffers because of the negative connotations of the name.  It is a superior shortening, and when locally sourced, far less detrimental than hydrogenated fats such as Crisco.
Reheated them the next morning and then topped them with icing. Great with coffee and scrambled eggs.
Filling was delicious; pastry was bland.

Made recipe as is but without sugar. 
Filling: 800 g spinach, two things feta, three things dill, one leek, one red onion, two cloves garlic, two eggs.

These empanadas were a bear to make with a rolling pin but, boy, were they the hit of a New Year's Eve gathering! Everyone loved the sweet, spicy filling and the dough. Since I discovered at the last minute that I didn't have enough shortening, I used half butter and half shortening in the dough. The pastry was soft, tasty, and a lovely color. Will definitely make again. Worth buying a tortilla press!

Cut onions into crescent-shaped slices. The best way to do this is to cut once in the middle holding the onion upright with the "root" at the bottom, and then placing each half cut-side down, followed by slicing top-to-bottom to produce crescent-shaped slices. Easy! This is a basic technique for a lot of Indian recipes including pulav.
Not a big fan of deep frying, can this be made in an air fryer or baked (drizzle oil while baking).
Absolutely! I air fry 6 minutes each side or till they are golden brown.

Made this as written and fried in peanut oil. Delicious!  Just the right amount of spice. Make sure to not compress the onion mixture too much, you want it loose so it gets crispy. This is the best version of “pakoras” that I have ever made.

Curious: vertical is best IMO
Then cut sections . Like an orange

I absolutely eschew the unworthy “canola” oil. It’s unhealthy, viz., inflammatory, untasty, undelightful; & whoever has heard of a canola plant? Canola is a corporate acronym. I adore fried onions, & if you get the oil/ghee/cooking method right, you will be transported to nirvana with onion bhaji. Delights await!
Canola is the oil of the rapeseed plant. Is it any wonder the name was changed? For what it’s worth, I know someone who farmed those plants in Canada (the “can” in “canola” iirc)

Canola is a rebrand/re-name of rapeseed as noted by T Jones. The 'rape' is a derivative of rabe, or turnip (similar etymology to kohl-rabi) as the plant is a brassica with bright yellow flowers frequently seen in fields through Europe and Asia.

It is one of the oldest known vegetable oils, with modern varieties bred to reduce the erucic acid content that was recognised as a problem in the 1970s.
Are the sweet onions to be halved vertically or horizontally?
I use a mix of red and yellow onions. To slice the onions, Enthusiast has described it accurately.
I only have glutinous rice flour -- any chance that will work?  (I do have chickpea flour, though!)

I’ve made these for years and never used rice flour at all.  Just use your gram flour /chickpea flour & they will be délicieux.
NB: if you only have dried chickpeas in your larder, any good blender will turn them into flour - just blitz them cup at a time until the flour is fine enough.

My Indian mother-in-law makes these but she cuts the onion much smaller, and makes little crispy deep-fried balls that are perfect dipped into cilantro chutney. Whether it is traditional I can't comment on, but they are delicious and much more appetizing than the larger strips of onion called for in this recipe.
Made these a few weeks ago. House still has that delightful smell of onions being fried.
If you can find whole cumin seeds, toast them in a pan with whole black pepper seeds and the whole coriander seeds before crushing them in the mortar and pestle to enhance the flavor.

title: Ayote en Miel (Squash With Spiced Syrup) Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: For cooks asking about source for panela (brown sugar cane - piloncillo or dulce de atado), Google or Amazon both come in handy if you're stumped. Piloncillo is a Mexican brown sugar cane that is in sort of a cone shape and available at Mexican markets well stocked standard groceries. Even food giant Goya sells it - including at mass retailers like Target for about $1.30 / 8 oz cone - but there are others too. https://www.thespruceeats.com/how-to-use-piloncillo-2343039

This recipe also works for candied sweet potatoes.  Peel them and make as directed for the squash.  If the potatoes are cooked before the syrup has thickened, remove them from the pot and cook the syrup down to the desired consistency.
You can find Panela in any grocery store in Mexican food aisle. It’s that brown cone thing.

I made this today, and it is delicious. However, I am baffled by the timing in the recipe. After dissolving the panela (I used jaggery, same-same) I added butternut squash and cooked, covered, for 45 minutes. At that point it was almost falling apart and clearly if I had left it in the syrup much longer it would have dissolved into soup. So I took it out, placing the pieces in a ceramic lasagna pan. Then I boiled the syrup down to a lovely thick caramel-like sauce, and poured it over the squash pieces. It’s great, I’m delighted, everything’s fine. But no way could it have survived 2 hours of simmering. And a Cinderella Pumpkin wouldn’t have lasted any longer. So I’m mystified. Anyway, 5 stars, 2 thumbs up, go for it!

@Amanda Burton
I wished I checked the comments sooner! My Cinderella pumpkin entirely dissolved :( Still delicious though— I think I’ll use it as a filling for something!
Piloncillo is easily found at most CTown or Key food markets in NYC in the international food sections.

It is a lot of sugar, but you can try it with less. I have made something similar called simply calabasa. It also uses piloncillo, and you can find it easily, but it looks rough and crude. I bought mine wrapped in a corn leaf. Super sweet, but filled with the bounty of the harvest. It's probably more of a Northern harvest in Mexico. I learned this recipe from my mother-in-law, and her heritage points in that direction. She adds other small fruits and nuts, boiling down the calabasa.

Don't make the mistake I made - it seemed like so much sugar, and since I was using the low-end of the spectrum in terms of squash, I figured I'd reduce the sugar. Maybe fine, but I reduced the water proportionally too--OOPS! I thought I had it on a pretty low temperature, but I completely burned the bottom of everything. Scraped off what I could salvage and now have some smoky-sweet pumpkin mash that I might use in a recipe asking for puree! But did not get to try the desired result.

This recipe, while delicious, has a cook time that’s a bit too long. If you caught it too late like I did, fret not. Keep the heat steady and you’ll be rewarded with a marvelous pumpkin butter. 
I think using very large chunks (larger than recommended) will give you the best bet of getting the expected dessert :)
Would Delicata squash work?

bjw,  This won't work with Delicata squash.  It requires a dense, heavy squash.  Although I haven't yet tried this Salvadorian recipe, I have made a very similar Mexican version (calebaza en tacha) a couple of times.  Heavy squash, like edible pumpkin (not carving pumpkin) works best.  The recipe ingredient list also suggests butternut.
What can we substitute for the sugar, which is a new ingredient to me? Is that found in Mexican grocery stores?
My local boring old Safeway sells panela in the Mexican section, and sometimes next to the tiny display of Central/South American fruits in the produce section. Look and you might find it!
Are there substitutes for the sugar, which is a new ingredient to me? Where can we find it?
If it helps panela can be found in many Latin markets. It's similar to dark brown sugar. It's usually sold in a solid disk. Bet Amazon carries it if you don't have a good bodega near you.
Can brown sugar be used as a substitute for panela?

Confused about this recipe?
Pause and consider:
There's squash, which is delicious on it's own, plus sugar and spices.
If you want to add sugar, there are many options (brown, white, maple, honey, HFCS, etc).
Confused about the 1.5lbs of sugar cane? Use what you have instead, in an amount that makes sense to you.
See also: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat; food recipes usually aren't as exacting as metallurgical alloy recipes, for example. Some baking recipes may require exact amounts.
I added star anis, ginger and lemon peel to the syrup. Delicious.

Thinking about making this for Thanksgiving, but I’m concerned with a burner being occupied for 2.5 hours. Is it possible to at least do the first 1.5 hour of cooking ahead of time and then the last hour prior to mealtime? Or, will that ruin the “syrup?”
Really that much sugar…

I was too lazy to go to the Mexican grocery store for dulce de rapadura, so I just used dark brown sugar and Kabocha squash from Sprouts. The squash skin was soft, so I didn't stir it, but the recipe turned out just like the ayote en miel I used to get in Honduras.
This recipe is a combination of chilly and suger but yeah i loved it and enjoyed a lot.

The linked source for online Djon-Djon is $28/oz. 
With no real flavor profile provided in the article, does anyone with experience with these mushrooms have an economical substitute?
I'd love to see an answer to this. You can find the bouillon on Amazon and it is much cheaper than the dried mushrooms.
Kalustyan's, in NYC, has them on their website, $14.99 for 1.5 oz.

Etsy sells 125 grams, which is 4.4 ounces, for $26.

The recipe on haitiancooking.com says toss the mushrooms after soaking and boiling

I've made lots of djon-djon rice, introduced by my Haitian neighbor. There's no substitute for djon-djon. Woodsy and floral. Unique fragrance and flavor. I've tried other types (wood-ear, shiitake, etc.) -- not even in the ballpark. I use pigeon peas not lima beans, and keep blended epis frozen. Maggi djon-djon cubes for mushrooms are like top-ramen chicken packets for homemade stock. Or just slightly better than that. The mushroom's central to the dish. Support Haitian self-determination.
I found djon djon on Amazon for $12.20 an ounce--much better buy!

As a substitute, I’d use either the dried black mushrooms from Eastern Europe that are used in Ashkenazi Jewish soups, or dried shiitake mushrooms. These mushrooms can be reserved after soaking and used in other dishes, unlike djondjon.

Regarding your question about the mushrooms, we do not add them back into this traditional recipe. We only use the stock they were cooked in.

Thank you for your question and for your interest in our traditional recipe.

I’d substitute the Eastern European black mushrooms that are used in certain Ashkenazi Jewish soups. The next choice would be dried shiitake. Both of these mushrooms can be saved and used in other dishes after soaking.

Alternatively, if you know anyone in S. Florida, perhaps they can buy djondjon for you at a Haitian grocery. I stocked up every time I visited my late parents in W. Palm Beach.
Are the mushrooms being added back to the rice? Or not ?

Hi Ludovic,

I agree that the great black color of the djon djon is not reflected in the picture. It may be due to the lighting, but I can assure you that the recipe will produce a perfectly black djon djon if followed carefully.

Thank you for your feedback.
Are you supposed to discard the mushrooms after making the broth? There’s no mention of adding them back to the completed dish.

This is great recipe . My on my questions is if we are adding back the mushroom to the rice dish?
Thank you
Nicole
No you are not.
You wouldn't believe how good are the dried mushrooms from Costco. They keep forever and are so economical.

For a substitute for djon djon you can use MAGGI-Djon-bouillon-cubes-they can found on amazon.  And, some ppl use both the mushroom and the cubes.  By the way, many Haitian cooks, especially those from the North, would find the amount of djon djon specified in this recipe to be insufficient. The resulting rice for them should be black, not a muddy gray.

title: Pasta con Palta (Creamy Avocado Pesto Pasta) Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: Followed recipe exactly, except that I first lightly sautéed the garlic in the olive oil to mellow its sharpness. I also tossed pasta & avocado dressing with halved cherry tomatoes and canned corn kernels for some color and texture contrast. Huge hit with my 4 dinner guests. Rich, creamy, comforting, and beautiful on the plate. Warning: recipe as written makes 4 LARGE main course servings. I’d halve this for 4 adults as a side dish. We had leftovers, which darkened unattractively within an hour

Soak minced garlic in lemon juice soften the garlic's sharpness for 15 minutes, then gently fry the garlic in olive oil in a skillet.  Mash up your avocado(s) like you would for guac--no lumps.  Cook pasta to al dente in salted water but less water than normal to concentrate the starch -- this will help make the sauce extra creamy.  When the pasta is ready, dump it in the skillet with garlic using tongs. Add avocado and toss add a bit of the pasta water and toss until nice and creamy.
I make something similar, but I always add basil (preferably freshly picked) and a little lemon juice.

I make this with avo with a generous squeeze of lemon, then I serve with well-roasted (in my airfryer) cherry tomatoes, tossed in a a splash of olive oil and balsamic. The sweet, slightly charred little tomatoes are a nice foil for the lemony avo, and makes a complete meal.

Having just made this, next time I'd absolutely skip the blender (a ripe avocado is too soft and light to blend well). Mash the avocado with a fork. Crush the walnuts with a mallet in a plastic bag. Stir them together with the olive oil and minced garlic. Definitely reserve the pasta water to coat. (I added lemon juice and red pepper flakes for more flavor, too.)
I will probably make this with unsalted pepitas or sunflower seeds, because that's what I have, not walnuts. And I'm sure it will be great.
I also make a variation of this with lemon, basil, and goat cheese and any type of nut (have tried cashews, pistachios, pine nuts - all work). It doesn’t darken and is delicious!

Hmm - I've been married into a Chilean family for 25 years. They are predictable fanatics when it comes to "palta," but no one has ever heard of putting it on pasta. One thing I can say is that if this were made in Chile it would definitely have lemon mixed into the palta. The addition of tomatoes would be very authentic as well. And then they'd skip the pasta and smear the whole thing on bread with a nice bottle of red, which is very Chilean.
@ACK yes, as a Chilean, the mention of “palta” caught my attention, since it’s a Chilean name for avocado. But I also, had never heard of putting this on pasta ever

I used a short pasta (campanelle) instead of long, and it really held the sauce beautifully. I added some lemon zest and juice from half a lemon, which brightened everything up.  Also added chopped zucchini to the pasta in the last few minutes of cooking. The sweetness of the zucchini was a lovely balance to the other flavors. This was divine!

Think it would be better if they avocado and walnuts were pieces and you tossed olive oil
with pasta and then added avocado and nuts.

It was ok, nothing spectacular but interesting. I follow the recipe exactly, didn't change a thing. I just took advice of some cooks that it would be better to  sautéed the garlic with I did. 
I don't think I would cook it again, but just in case, I saved it. Who knows
Thankfully, someone who made the recipe as written and reported back!  More helpful than everyone who invented their own variation (ie, a different recipe).

So boring!  I don’t recommend this. I can’t even think what might make it better. It needs something to add some zing- maybe lemon or cilantro or Parmesan but it’s so delicate I think those would overwhelm the avocado flavor. 

Not worthy of the pasta we tossed it with.

@SPWMS: Sure— change the nuts. My personal pick of the nuts you mentioned would be pine nuts (generally pasta friendly) or cashews (richer), but go with what appeals to you. Almonds will add a sweeter flavor, if that is what you prefer.
I followed the recipe religiously (except for market not Hass avocados) and diners thought the result delicious.
For anyone who’s slightly confused, the more common Spanish word for avocado is “aguacate,” derived from the Aztec name. In the Southern Cone of South America the common word for avocado is “palra.”
Very good. I added a little lemon to combat discoloration. Very creamy.  I used a blender and would opt for a food processor next time.

Reading the recipe it reminded me very much of dishes that I had while in Chile - simple, but delicious. However, it relies on all the ingredients being exceptionally fresh and of great quality. Sadly, grocery store avocados - or at least the ones I can get my hands on in Virginia - are never going to fit that bill. I made as written and discovered it to be very bland. So I added some sun dried tomatoes and some cojito cheese to punch it up.
Added sautéed mushrooms and Gorgonzola because I had them on hand, and it was amazing!

Excellent base recipe. Added basil and toasted pine nuts (pesto for me = basil, garlic and pine nuts) instead of walnuts, blended half with the avocado and scattered the rest amongst the pasta.  Lemon juice, goat cheese and a few red pepper flakes into the avocado mix as well. Lights out good. Guest worthy.

As written, the recipe makes a bland, flat dish, and requiring a blender to make it is ridiculous. I added lemon juice and zest to help it out. Not enough. I’ll add tomatoes and some pepper heat if I ever make it again. Maybe greens or corn or some protein. And I’ll crush the garlic and nuts then add them to a smashed avocado. I halved the recipe, and it’s enough for 3.
I hate to leave such a harsh review, but this recipe was truely abhorrent. Just don't make it.
I’m Chilean and never grew up eating palta with pasta..we do eat it with everything else though. That being said, I love this combo! Just not sure it’s accurate to say it’s Chilean.
Next time I’ll try to process with lemon to balance the fat and herbs first some flavor and moisture

I recently started to buy avocados in bulk and freeze them.  I let them ripen, cut in quarters to easily and safely pit them, drop the quarters in lemon juice that I then drain off them and place them in freezer bags, 2 avocados/bag.  When they thaw, they are a bit mushy, perfect for this recipe, a rough guacamole, or mashed as a sandwich garnish.  My new fav hack and my new fav recipe.
Very disapointing result and a waste of all the ingredients used.
I substituted cashews once and almonds the other - both delish. Also added lemon juice and Basil. The creamiest was a huge hit. Served with shrimp sautéed in butter n garlic.
Agree with Violet in adding fresh basil & lemon juice.
Made this last nite for dinner.  It’s very nice, but imho it needs another texture— maybe some additional chopped walnuts or toasted pinko for a bit of crunch.  Otherwise so simple and good!
If I were to leave walnuts out of the recipe, would it be noticeable ? Could cashews, pine nuts or almonds be a decent sub ?
This sounds great. But I can't eat nuts. What could I use instead?
In other pestos, I've substituted pepitas (pumpkin seeds without the shell) for nuts.  You could probably also use sunflower seeds.
Adding the lemon juice at the end improves the dish enormously. Made it along with roasted broccoli to add some texture and it worked well enough.
Would using another kind of avo matter?
What are substitutes for walnuts if you have an allergy?

I'm always amused by the comments which just *have* to amend the recipe. A not exactly subtle way of saying "I know so much better than the author." If tomatoes are added it is no longer pasta con pasta.

I made it as written.  I don't have the aversion to garlic that most seem to and just use it raw and with it's full flavor.  I agree that it would be better with some cherry tomatoes and I was wondering how to roast them.  It was a good, lite dish but was missing something.
Instead of viewing it as 'I know so much better' try people making it their own.  Many people in the cooking world promote this sort of thing and view it as how we bring joy or satisfaction to mundane task of life.
We didn't love the recipe. A bit bitter and mushy.
based on other notes, added cherry tomatoes and fresh basil.  Also, a bit more salt and red pepper flakes.  Fast weekday dinner.

Followed recipe exactly.  Didn’t add quite enough pasta water.  More would make it more creamy.  Next time I’ll add the squeeze of lemon juice and cherry tomatoes.  Not sure sautéed garlic is worth the effort.  Depends on sensitivity to raw garlic in a pesto.  (I’ll know tomorrow!)
The avocado and pasta were bland together and I wanted to put on grated cheese and pancetta bits to liven it up. Pesto is better made with a zesty green and I would not make this again.

Think pasta with guacamole, guacamole that doesn’t have spice or citrus in it.
When a recipe is made of ingredients that are favorites, i think it might be a winner, but not this time.  I tried to doll it up with some sumac which did improve it a little.

Finally got around to trying this. Followed the recipe using freshly opened vacuum-packed walnut pieces and ripe avocados. The walnuts were bitter (and I love walnuts), the garlic was bitter, and I couldn't taste much avocado. I agree with some others that this makes too much. Won't make again

Read this recipe and thought: hmm, sounds good but not too sure. Gave it a try. Cut the recipe in half for two, made sauce in the blender, where it was pretty sludgy despite the addition of two  tablespoons of tap water and a teaspoon of lemon juice in hopes of getting it moving. No luck, so when pasta was done I added half a cup of pasta water, which smoothed it right out to a perfect consistency. 1/2

2/2 Added halved fresh cherry tomatoes and half a cup of thawed frozen corn and a pinch of hot chili flakes when I tossed the sauce with the pasta. Knew it was a winner when husband asked, “Is there more?”

I found that the dish prepared as written was too bland for my taste. I think lemon or lime juice could brighten the flavor and help maintain the avocado color. I agree with others that adding crushed nuts when serving the dish would enhance the appeal and make the texture more appealing.

Unfortunately we did not love this. I’m not sure if it was the raw garlic - I see other commentators have taken steps to mellow the flavor - but this tasted somewhat fishy to us. If we make again, I will likely follow suggestions to either sauté the garlic or soak in lemon juice, and perhaps not use the food processor.

I followed the recipe exactly as written and found it to be not only bland but very flat tasting, Generally I love avocados but this was definitely missing anything that would make it either memorable or even repeatable.  It isn’t that it’s bad, and it could be improved with added ingredients, but why bother.  For me this is not a keeper!

The sauce never blended in my blender.  In the end I added some of the pasta water in the blender, but it still wasn't smooth as there just wasn't enough liquid to get going in the blender.  It tastes pretty good, but had chunks of walnut.  Also the raw garlic taste was pretty strong.  If I I cook this again I will use my mini food processor and saute the garlic in the olive oil first.
Followed the recipe exactly and the result was... bland. Just pasta in a bland pale green sauce.

Creamy, nutty, super! Surprised it didn't call for cheese...added a glug of soy sauce (like I do for EVERYTHING) and it was deeelicious. Satisfying enough with a big salad to be Meatless Monday and nicely fatty enpugh to be filling and satisying. Yum.

A bland dish based on the bare-bones NYT recipe, made flavorful by the addition of several basil leaves (thanks, NYT commenters!) and a generous dose of grated parmesan, which I blended into the sauce after my first taste.
Prepared it as the recipe said, very bland! Added some pesto, lemon, and parmesan which helped a little but it was still a very bland dish overall.

I made this for a quick lunch today, topped with some chopped tomatoes and a squeeze of lemon juice.  Tasted good but aesthetically much more bland looking than the photo.  Next time I might add a green herb (basil, cilantro, mint, or parsley) or a few fresh spinach leaves to the sauce to give it some color.

Grate some garlic, mash up an avocado or two, squeeze in some lime juice, add salt & pepper then stir in hot pasta.  One of my go to quick dinners.  Sprinkling on some nuts sounds like a fine addition.

-I would use a food processor over a blender if possible. I tried using a blender (because I was too lazy to pull out the Cuisinart) and it really didn't work.
-A similar recipe on NYT Cooking uses a little lemon juice; I think that would brighten up the flavor a little and perhaps curtail some of the darkening of color (esp. for leftovers)
-As noted in another comment, the avocado will darken in an unappealing way, over time. Don't make many leftovers!

I wish I had read the  comments before making this. I definitely would have fried up the garlic. Straight garlic is hard for me to handle.
This was quite bland. I would have added cherry tomatoes and parmesan cheese.

Quick, easy and very good.  We liked the creamy texture.  My only change was to add a couple of teaspoons of lemon juice.  For subsequent batches I'll experiment with chunkier texture, cheeses, tomatoes, etc.

I loved this, was so comforting. I didn’t have walnuts or a blender so I ground the garlic and some flaked almonds in a pestle and mortar before adding in the avocado. Stirred through some rocket I had handy towards the end as well. Delicious and peppery.

Yum. Make half sauce and half pasta and still be enough for 4 people. Added half a lemon worth of lemon zest. Used bow tie pasta. Served with grilled boneless skinless thighs - marinated half day in lemon, garlic, olive oil and Aleppo marinade - lemon heavy small amount total

Not enough flavor, though Mom liked it a lot. I sprinkled red pepper flakes before serving, that helped. Used lots of squeezed lemon, which I saw in notes of others. Worth trying again because I think there's room to be creative and make it better.

I have to say, as written this is very bland. Barely edible. After I tasted, first added red pepper flakes.  Then added parmesan.  Then more salt.  Then tomatoes on the vine (all I had).  The tomatoes improved it considerably, but it really needs lemon.  I would add lemon next time, but don't think I will make again.  Better to make some guac, and just have pasta with olive oil and cheese.

Must add acid-lemon, lime, whatever you have on hand. Definitely add the full cup of pasta water. It was plenty of sauce to coat 20 oz. Of pasta. It is so rich; I think it would be better as a side dish.
Careful with walnuts (can be too nutty) & add lots of salt to balance out
added lime juice and Bragg's aminos,  Bingo.

For someone with an allergy to nuts -- how about adding a tablespoon of nutritional yeast? Just looking for alternatives, but I'm sure it can be made without nuts altogether. I do think a squeeze of lemon would be a good idea.
Made with toasted pecans, not walnuts (too bitter); and used a lemon/salt/pepper blend, and lemon infused oil, after reading others’ suggestions.
Great easy recipe.  I did add the corn and tomatoes and definitely added to the pasta.  I will reduce the amount of garlic next time.

This was way better than I thought it was going to be and so simple. I added the juice and zest of a lemon to it and make sure you use plenty of black pepper. The walnut was really nice to add a little complexity and I garnished with the lemon zest and some rough chopped walnut for a little crunch. 

I went heavier on the avocado ratio to make the sauce extra thick compared to the picture on this recipe showed. This would likely be really nice with a piece of cooked chicken breast and/or some halved/quartered cherry tomatoes as well. 

Worked awesome in my immersion blender processor attachment. No mess and crazy quick to make.
Added shallots and green peas iso walnuts to make it a bit lighter. More taste with lemon zest &thyme added and topped it of with biological smoked salmon.

title: Arroz con Maiz y Crema (Cheesy Rice Casserole With Corn) Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: This makes so much (also true with a lot of NY Times Cooking recipes). How well does it cut in half? Sure wish there were more smaller serving recipes for us singletons.
I don't see why you couldn't half this recipe. Just bake it in a smaller dish for a shorter time.
Patrick, this recipe can be reduced very easily into halves or thirds.  Just use a suitably sized dish for baking.

I just noticed they even say in the description: "A satisfyingly easy side dish that is rich, cheesy and tangy, this casserole can be prepared and assembled in advance, or easily halved for a smaller crowd."

My taste buds got into a knock-down, drag-out fight with my coronary arteries over this recipe.  A truce was negotiated that provided for: small servings, low-fat ingredients (where/if possible), and a half mile run every night for a week after each serving.  
That said, I made a half sized version that was quite tasty, and will again.  Yum!
I will try this using mild green chilis (Ortega or Hatch), perhaps cilantro for parsley.
This was good and I passed it off as ‘Rainbow Casserole’ to my little kids.  Added 1/2 cup black beans.  I cut the mayo to 1/2 cup and the s cream to 1 cup.   Still gooey and yummy.
I like the idea of adding black beans.
Just wondering, does 50 minutes include time to cook rice? I don't know many people who have 6 cups of rice hanging out in the fridge.

If you use a rice cooker, making the rice is a matter of measuring rice and water and pushing a button. And if you can't get it going an hour before you'll need it for the recipe, you can make it a day or three in advance and refrigerate it until you need it!
@Su thank you! Cooked rice freezes well, so there's another option.

I cut the recipe in half, used light sour cream and greek youghurt instead of regular sour cream and mayo, and substituted a cup of Hatch green chiles (Young Guns is the best) for the bell peppers. I also added cumin, red pepper flakes, a dash of cayenne, and chile powder. Que rico!
Looks good.  Any ideas for replacing the red peppers?  My family hates them, (though I eat them when out...)
I am going to try and substitute pimiento as I read "they're great for recipes that call for chopped up bell peppers".
Maybe thin shavings of zucchini, tossed with a bit of EVOO and some herbs before being placed on top?  Canned hearts of palm?
Just leave them out?  They're probably in the recipe for color.
I routinely cook 3 cups of rice at a time for the two of us, and the next day put half in a freezer bag. I always have frozen rice for a casserole or to put a batch of curry on.
Pure, delicious comfort food. I made it with leftover brown basmati rice, and it was excellent.

This is so darn good!! Fair warning: I added to the recipe. But this recipe begs to be tinkered with! Added jalapenos, left-over adobo peppers and sauce. More corn — half I browned and caramelized, the other half still frozen. Delish!

You could try adding frozen green beans, green peas, frozen diced carrots, or even frozen chopped spinach. They all work well. well. Substitute green pepper (if they will eat that instead of the red pepper). Mushrooms or fresh zucchini are also good substitutes that I have used successfully for the red pepper hating eaters in my household.

Really shocked this doesn't have better reviews. I did take quite a few liberties as I only used what I had on hand. I halved the recipe because I'm only cooking for two. I used a can of diced green chilis in place of the bell pepper, granulated onion instead of scallions, and added some diced bacon. I also didn't have parsley and used some dried oregano instead. This all worked perfectly and was delicious -- I'm looking forward to making it again.

Halved this and it was delicious! My husband, who normally has no opinion on dinner, said that this recipe is a keeper. Made 1 cup of rice to yield 3 cups cooked, a day in advance. Leftover rice keeps it from being mushy. Used cilantro instead of parsley and firmly think it’s better that way. Used jarred roasted red peppers for ease. 
Full of flavor, but very rich. Paired it with the NYT cherry tomato and white bean salad for the vinegary contrast. Great pairing for a meatless dinner.
I don't see why you couldn't substitute drained chopped or canned tomatoes for the red peppers. That sounds really good to me!

Family called this Mexican Rice Kugel:
Tonight I made half a batch with leftover rice that was in the freezer, Greek nonfat yogurt, fresh corn, olive oil mayo, and a well roasted red pepper (roasted yesterday when I had more time). Dinner time was uncertain so I baked this a bit early and finished it under the broiler to brown up. 
Family loved it!
It worked really nicely with a brown rice/quinoa mix. And an added chopped spinach layer. Even the two year old liked it.
Any veggie to add color:  cherry or grape tomatoes (maybe cut in half), peas, edamame, etc.

I added chicken for some protein. 6 cups is way too much rice but I filled a 9x13 pan. I used two small cans of green chiles and spread that on the top-under the melty cheese cuz it was just so bland after mixing all together. I have enough rice left over to use for something else next week. Not the best from NYT but will fit the need for dinner with my in-laws.

I cut recipe in half. In my family, half recipe serves six, with side salad. Have made twice. Used frozen “fire-roasted” corn and brown rice. Once I added a poached, shredded half chicken breast, the other time I added smoked paprika. Both additions were good! I like broiling at the end, too brown the top.

Just wanting to extra emphasize that this is a side dish :)) I made it and enjoyed it with black beans and avocado, and it was so yummy! I 2/3s the recipe lol and with the accompaniments we have probably two-three more meals for the two of us! So yes it’s a lot. 

I also added a fresh green chili diced up in there for a little kick and did extra corn and peppers :P tasted totally fine with parm. Also did cilantro instead of parsley and it was great! We are overseas so we just kinda wung it.

If your kids don't like peppers I hide them by putting the mayo/sour cream in a small food chopper or blender and using jar pimentos or fresh red peppers and blending it all up to disquise the veggies.
I halved this and subbed plain yogurt for the sour cream. Liked it a lot.

Everyone loved this! I did cut back on the amount of rice and upped the amount of corn (frozen worked fine). Also, it felt like the dish was a bit like street corn in a casserole form, so I added chili powder and the juice from a whole lime. The addition of the lime juice was excellent.

This was disappointing for me. The whole thing had a sticky, mushy texture that was not appetizing and the flavors weren't very special. Like many people who left comments, I thought it would work and liked the ingredients. But in the end, this one didn't work here. Happy that others found success with it.

This was phenomenal. I used jasmine rice cooked with 1/2 bulb garlic & 1 onion (sauteed) in a rice cooker with recommended water and a healthy spoonful of roasted garlic and veggie better than bouillon. I never do plain rice.  Finished up manchego and cojita cheese, plus recommended cheese, used whole milk Cabot Greek yogurt in place of sour cream and I only use duke's mayo. Served with salsa verde chicken, slow cooked. yum!

I doubled the corn and halved the sour cream and mayonnaise. Loved the mix or flavors - especially the roasted red peppers and fresh parsley. I am going to add in some green chili or a little hot chili next time, but this makes a great side for a meal.
I added cubed ham with lime zest and lime juice as well as halving mayo, sour cheese and cheese- tasty
Delicious. Ignore baking time. Baked it for at least an hour - it’s dense and wet.

Made as directed (except I halved the recipe) and thought it was kind of bland, which disappointed me given the number of ingredients. If there's a next time, I might amp it up by adding some spices (maybe a little cumin and/or chili powder) and perhaps some pickled jalapeños. I see that some commenters have also added bits of meat which might also help things a bit.

A new favorite. I scrambled to find a GF recipe to add to the mix for an early Thanksgiving gathering, and this was perfect. I used what I had: reduced fat sour cream/Greek yogurt, olive oil mayo (fewer calories), canned shoepeg corn, brown rice, and asadero for the melting cheese; the rest is as written.  A unanimous thumbs up for future holidays. Next time? Hatch chilies, too!
Delicious and so easy with little clean up.
I used roasted poblanos that had turned red in the garden — so good & a decadent accompaniment the black bean & mushroom chili also on this site.
I added shredded rotisserie chicken and some diced and sautéed squash and zucchini. SO GOOD and the whole family loved it. Perfect cozy fall meal.

I halved this when I made it and it filled an 8" x 11" casserole dish nicely.

Also, I messed up slightly and mixed in the Parmesan instead of sprinkling it on top. (Still turned out great though.) Substituted Muenster cheese for Monterey Jack and grated that and put it on top. It melted and broiled superbly. Great dish!
Yes, use 4 ears of fresh corn.  I made it straight up, but added a can or chopped green chiles drained.  It was absolutely splendid.  I did put it under the broiler for 2 minutes at the end.

This was DELICIOUS.  Served it as a main dish with a bunch of fresh produce (tomatoes, avocados, watermelon, mango, and lime) on the side to balance things out nutritionally.  YUM.  The only change I made was substituting the jarred (canned?) red bell peppers.  Instead, I just charred, sweated, and peeled fresh red bell peppers, and finely chopped them.  I also might have included a bit more corn, scallion, parsley, and peppers than called for.  Anyway, so good.  YES.
Where is the meat?
How about substituting the peppers with capers or olives?

This recipe is a hit with my family.  It is easy to put together for a quick weeknight side that pairs well with a variety of veggie or meat mains.  I imagine the full recipe works well for a larger group.  I was also able to successfully scale it down for our family of three with plenty leftover.  This one is a keeper!

So easy and so good. I halved the recipe. I used chopped fresh red bell pepper because I didn't have jarred/roasted. I also added a can of diced green chilies per someone's suggestion. I served it with Sarah Copeland's Broiled Fish Tacos and some refried beans. It's a perfect meal in my book. I will make it again!
This was a really interesting side dish and we had it with ribs.  It felt like it needed some type of citrus but we liked this a lot - made exactly as written.
Rice takes 20 minutes to cook, and the casserole takes 30 minutes to bake, so yes, 50 minutes is accurate.

I think prep time should include yelling at your family to help you find the cheese grater and running to the store for extra cheese. This one was easy to cut in half. Didn't have sour cream, so I used yogurt. Cottage cheese might also work. Definitely add some Tajin or maybe some smoked chili powder.

This is a wonderful recipe. It can scale to serve 2-20. I first had a variation of this 55y ago; this version is great. Substitutions are simple. Precook the rice. If it's leftover and dry, adjust the mayo and sour cream. Whatever mild peppers you have will work (jarred red peppers or pimientos, roasted poblanos, or canned green chiles). Grated carrots add flavor, color, nutrition; corn too. Blends of cheddar & Jack cheese or Parmesan & goat add new flavors (haven't tried blue or swiss cheeses).

I’ve made this a couple of times now, never quite following the recipe exactly—adapted to what I had on hand—but it strikes me as a wonderful template for cheesy rice that’s a perfect base for experimentation and play—definitely a keeper

This can be a side dish OR meal. My mom served it at Christmas and it was a huge hit. When a friend fell and dislocated his shoulder we threw some cooked chicken in it and made it a meal. Cheesy, warm, and can easily be heated up with red pepper flakes or jalapeños!

Made with half brown rice half quinoa, 6 roasted poblanos 4 cups fresh corn and a zucchini. I didn't have sc so made a white sauce with butter flour and cream and added 1/3 c mayo. Added a little cumin red pepper flakes and cayenne. Delicious spicy and creamy.
Love this very versatile recipe. I add a little cayenne to the mixing bowl. I love that it works well with different cheeses. I ran out of mayo once and used extra sour cream and it tasted fine.
Add in cooked shredded chicken to make this a heartier meal! I boiled four chicken breasts, but you may be able to just shred a rotisserie chicken as well.

Have made this twice, the first time replacing the mayo with Greek yogurt and second time replacing the sour cream with Greek yogurt and adding 2 tsp of smoked paprika and some red onion. Enjoyed it both times.
Great recipe. I followed the recipe with no changes and it was great. It is very rich and creamy. But, it reheats nicely in the microwave as there were a lot of yummy leftovers.
This is really good.  Next time I'll go a little lighter on the jack cheese on top, though.  It probably doesn't need much.

Loved this! I made a half size portion with a few subs, due to lacking the exact ingredients- I used fresh bell pepper, finely chopped, subbed cottage cheese for sour cream, threw in a few tablespoons of Franks hot sauce,  plus added about 1 cup of chopped rotisserie chicken. Great recipe for the rotation!

Sounds great. I went to the fast assemble version. Layer tartine as follows.

Rub raw garlic on the toast. 
Butter the toast. 
Lay several fillets on top of butter. 
Grind a bit o pepper.
Cilantro/parsley chopped coarse 
Halve a tomato and squeeze out a juicy tomato over greens.FUN!! (Eat skin and hard pulp on the side).
Squeeze of lemon.
Drizzle Evoo
Sprinkle Maldon salt

Bottle of Viogner is nice with this Umami goodness
I whipped this together in a bowl with a tined fork (and a bit of elbow grease), avoiding the mess left over in a blender or food processor.
YUM!!!YUM YUM!! When I taste tested this I immediately began to brainstorm. “How can I incorporate this delicious buttery goodness into sauces, slather on a grilled steak, etc etc
It’s not that different from a salsa verde that uses butter instead of olive oil.

We were in Tokyo this summer and went to a restaurant where the chef cooked in front of us (he made a lot of Okinawa style okonomiyaki) One of his other specialties was a mozzarella cheese sandwich on milk bread grilled in anchovy butter. It was sublime and this version of anchovy butter would be fabulous.
@P.Kaye Hanifee I’m wondering if you can remember the name of the restaurant where you had that delicious sandwich? My husband and I are going to Japan this summer. Thank you.

To Michael from Maryland… don’t add the whole half of lemon. Recipe tells you to take half a lemon..,. Zest it into the other ingredients… then squeeze in one teaspoon of lemon juice.  

Set aside remaining lemon for other use or discard.
I’m thinking I might make a batch and freeze it in smaller portions. Butter freezes perfectly but I am wondering about the anchovies within the mixture. Any thoughts on this?
Anchovy paste might make it easy to blend with the remaining ingredients. Figure 1/2 Tsp. paste is the equivalent of 1 anchovy fillet.
Easy to prep in a bowl without dirtying food processor. Went heavy on the anchovies. Not surprisingly it was even better the day after, when flavors had blended. I can imagine many uses.
I used end of season fresh basil which gave the anchovies sweetness. I'd  reduce the amount of butter in the future. Delicious with roasted potatoes.

Ess. 

It does freeze wonderfully!
Used high fat, high quality local butter. Added a pinch of kosher salt. Spread on a slice of artisan multigrain bread. *Chef’s kiss*

Try using lovage instead of parsley. I started growing lovage this year after reading about ancient Roman recipes. It is a delightful Erbe unfortunately is a perennial. It grows well even in Boston's wet and cold climate. It works beautifully in this recipe, adding a less aggressive flavor but still plenty of fresh herb taste.
Made it and loved it! I cant wait to slather on steak & crsty bread.
Does anyone understand the lemon? Really add half a lemon? Or just the zest?
Could I sub anchovy paste for the fillets?
Double or triple the quantity of anchovies to give the recipe more punch...
Smoked mackerel pate (smoked mackerel, butter and lemon juice) freezes perfectly, so I'm sure this would, too.
Fabulous. Worth cleaning the blender.
Because butter is not allowed for me,  I might try combining these ingredients with white beans.   Any tips are welcomed.
My new favorite savory spread on thin sourdough toast - such a sharp and salty buttery treat
I love this soooo much!!! This a forever recipe in my  family now!

I made this using curly parsley instead of flat-leaf (limited options at my market that day) and loved it. It's delicious on crusty, toasted bread, obviously. But I think I'm going to try it on pasta. I can also imagine it would be fantastic on chicken. Wonderful combination of flavors.

Ess. 


It freezes perfectly well!!!!
This is great! I will probably always have some on hand. Used the whole tin of anchovies. The blender was a pain. Cuisinart mini chop would be best.
can I do this with dill instead? my garden is busting with it. looking for creative uses
Used shallots instead of garlic. Delicious on steaks.
Fridge staple now

title: Purê de Mandioca (Creamy Yuca Purée) Recipe
author: Alexa Weibel

comments.commentBody: This seems to me to qualify as one of Sam Sifton’s “no recipe” recipes. I add garlic, olive oil, and grated lemon zest.

Yuca is a favorite of mine, being a sustainable nutritionist. It grows well with no agro toxins and needs no fertilizer made from fossil fuels.  Yuca doesn´t do well as a puré though; in puréed form it becomes pasty/gummy.  It is fantastic, though, boiled and then roasted with olive oil and garlic and served with lemon zest, as David Morris suggests.
It is amazing pureed. It is fufu in African countries.
I love mashed yuca, but there is no better French fry than a yuca one!
I completely agree.  It even beats sweet potato fries.  Pair with a dipping sauce of olive oil, garlic, salt and onion, and it is heavenly.
As a lactose intolerant Brazilian, adding milk is sacrilegious. I prefer just butter, which is lactose free,

The ratio of milk to yuca seems wrong. I used a pound of peeled and cleaned yuca, drained all water, added the butter, but with a bit over 1/2 cup of milk the mix became too soupy, that's not how we eat yuca purée in Brazil.
We liked this recipe but it was a little labor-intensive using a potato masher; next time I would use a food processor to smooth out the lumps.
@Rich C. I only make it in a food processor. Add a little olive oil to help with stickiness. Enjoy!
Poisonous???  Please explain.

Yuca naturally contains cyanide, which fortunately is water soluble. Different varieties have different amount of cyanide but all must be treated. Cut off the ends back about an inch. Peel and wash the peeled root under running water. Soak if you want. Boil, discarding the water as above. Then add whatever flavorings you want. In Thailand yuca is boiled again in sugar syrup, smashed, and then grilled. I like the Spanish American versions better

Used 1.5 lbs frozen bag with the rest of the normal amounts. A tiny thick but great.  Added the cheese, cilantro, parsley and black pepper and served it with the mushroom tin din from this website. Fantastic combination
chicken stock instead of bouillon cubes acceptable?
Very good, excellent flavor! Better than mashed potatoes. Will make this again.
My family (Dominican Republic) makes it 3 parts yuca, 1 parts potato, for a softer, soufflé-like finish. Yummy!
Croquetas are a classic dish from Spain that became part of the Cuban culinary tradition.
The real recipe does not uses crackers but bread (what you call french bread) when it's hard as a rock after a few days. Then you can grind it with a food processor to get the crumbs.
There is no "real" recipe.

When I lived in Spain 40 years ago, I learned to make ham, tuna, chorizo or cod croquettes for my family from an old pueblo woman in Campo Real.  There were no refrigerator or freezer pauses.  Just make the dough quite stiff, then grab a small handful, roll it, coat with bread crumbs and deep fry.  I also learned how to make empanadillas with some of the same proteins, summered in homemade pesto.  To this day, neither me nor anyone I’ve ever made these for have had one better.
This is the first recipe I’ve ever seen that calls for curly parsley. The flavor and texture of flat parsley is superior so I can’t imagine why curly is called for here.
@Purvis Must be because it’s the kind of parsley used in Spain.
I make these with a tablespoon or so of dry sherry in the ham mixture. It brings them up a notch.
Can I bake the croquettes ?  What oven temperature and for how long?

You actually can! 15-20 min at 200º Celsius, turning them once to cook and brown them evenly. Best if you drizzle some olive oil on them.  That said, they are better fried, and here in Spain you probably wouldn't find them oven baked (or with hot sauce and lime on top, lol).
Air fryer instructions?
I instruct you not to use an air fryer.
8-10 minutes at 395, turn once in between.  You’re welcome
Agreed, classic Spanish recipe, can sub any cooked protein- I have had w salmon, chicken, chorizo…
Classic CUBAN recipe for all those haters out there. If you want to get technical, Ham croquettas actually originated from France. So there, appreciate the recipe and move on.
Any advice on doing this in an air fryer?
Thank you for this recipe. My husband and I usually dish out lots of money at upscale Spanish restaurants to get these and there just a simple street food.
Any thoughts on trying this in an air fryer? Feasible? Temp/time?
Mother made turkey croquettes from thanksgiving leftovers. There’s a recipe in the joy of cooking. So delicious.

These were delicious.  I used oyster crackers to make my cracker crumbs because there is apparently a saltine shortage in my part of the country. I never got my white sauce to turn beige or brown even though I cooked it much longer than was called for but it got plenty thick and I just went ahead and used it. No complaints.

Croquetas are best with *ketchup*! Never in my 36 years of Cuban American life have I seen them served with lime and hot sauce, though I’m sure it’s delicious. Try them crispy and hot and squished between (preferably Cuban) bread with ketchup and a slice of cheese (Swiss is great). Perfect.
Croquetas are best with lime!

My cuban grandmother taught me how to make these as a child. She loved ham but would also make chicken and fish versions.  We always took frozen bags of croquetas on vacation and made sandwiches with them.  Brings back so many wonderful memories!

I don't think that I've ever had anything like this before, and I can't imagine the texture. They sound unappetizingly dense, but I'm sure that folks are raving about them for a good reason. Could someone describe what kind of texture is the goal here?

Sue, I've eaten these MANY times during the 25 years I lived in Spain, and they are delicious. Not unappetizingly dense at all, but smooth and creamy in the middle, crunchy on the outside. I remember having these at my (Spanish) boss's house for lunch when we were entertaining a distinguished visitor from the UK. She could not guess what they were made of. "Mashed potatoes, perhaps?" They can be made with ham, fish, chicken, ground beef... many possibilities. (Time consuming, though.)

The flavor on these was okay, but there was far too much flour in the roux. Mine was like a ball in the pan! I am going to try again and use a little over half the flour and see how that goes. Because it balled up, I wasn't able to brown it properly, and the finished croquetas were floury. Trying again this week and will report back.
Trying to use up some leftover Christmas ham.  Could these be frozen at the step prior to breading do you think?

y por que honey ham! why honey ham???
I always made with spanish ham or a ham steak cubed and lightly -very lightly- peppered.
Buy deli honey - mi pobre abuela-
Forget breasts, use pounded boneless thighs. So much better!

Bad advice.  Chicken parmesan uses chicken breasts.  Thighs are greasy, slimey,don't pound flat, and have a disgusting texture.  I feed them to my dog who doesn't like them either.  It's the worst part of the chicken.  There are so many recipes for thighs because for decades retailers didn't know what to do with them - no one ever bought them.  Having prevailed on recipe writers, retailers now get rid of the thighs and at exorbitant prices.  A well brined breast is preferable on all counts.
I cook gluten free. I use crushed rice chex or corn chex cereal in place of panko and/or bread crumbs and it comes out just fine!

This recipe ends up being VERY bland. I would add some salt, pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, chili flakes, etc to the bread crumb mixture. Once you do that it's perfect! We have a Cuisinart Convection Toaster Oven and I had to add maybe 6 minutes onto both the just chicken timing and the chicken+cheese timing. It was delicious!
Tough to find those smaller, more tender chicken breasts

I took the suggestions regarding adding more flavor and this turned out great.  And not all air fryers are the same so it paid off for me to know from previous attempts with different recipes how to adjust mine.

This was great. The whole meal came together in about 30 minutes, and the chicken breasts tasted like they had been marinated: all I did was pound them to an even thickness and followed the directions. Thank you!

Christina Morales

The breasts sold in grocery stores are huge, If I’m forced to use them, I cut them down to size for the dish I’m making then stew the unused trimmings, chop them into bite sized pieces, and freeze them for chicken salad, casseroles, etc.
My grocery store stocks thinly sliced chicken breasts, conveniently packaged. They work beautifully for chicken schnitzel/parm, etc. With a little pounding, they come out very tender.
I don't have an air fryer, but my new oven has an air fryer setting.  Does it do the same thing?  Would this (and other air fryer) recipe work in the oven set at air fryer?

Diana - 
I have had good results using the convection mode on my new oven in place of an air fryer. Give that air fryer mode a try!

The main feature of an “air fryer” is a very potent fan. (You’ll DEFINITELY hear a difference from the regular convection speed your oven likely uses.) By moving air around rapidly, rather than dipping food in oil, you’re getting rapid exposure to a lot of heat, thus simulating the effect of frying with less (or no) oil.
what about regular oven with air fryer features/

I made this tonight! It was so much quicker than the traditional method and the chicken breasts were so juicy. My air fryer has a broil function so I did that at the end.  The cheese got brown and bubbly. Next time, I’ll use a higher quality or homemade sauce.

The breasts were too large ones so I cut them in half, ending up with four pieces.  I didn't have Italian Seasoning and improvised.  I used a lot of oregano and garlic.  I had to cook the chicken longer than suggested.  2 minutes more before the cheese/sauce. And 2 minutes more with cheese and sauce.  I also cooked at 370 instead of 350 in my air fryer.  The chicken was tender, crispy on the outside and delicious. Used TJ's Tomato Basil Sauce. BIG HIT
Wonderful results, love this recipe, especially with my homemade marinara!

Double the sauce recipe

Tasty and easy to make. As others have noted, make sure to season your breadcrumb mixture well. I would also suggest a spritz of oil after breading to help it brown better.

This is a go-to recipe. I live in Europe and we have no chicken parmesan and I miss it from the Italian store I used to live across from in New Haven.

I make some modifications:
I use Ritz crackers for the crumb instead of the bread crumbs/panko. They have more oil and brown better.

I add a lot of garlic powder and double the Italian seasoning.

Can you use a convection oven instead for this recipe?
Since an air-fryer is just a mini-convection oven, I would think that would work just fine.
Yes, but it may help to use a slightly higher temperature and/or a slightly longer cooling time.   Air fryers are small, hot, fast convection ovens.
An "air fryer" IS a convection oven. So, yes! :-)
If you fold a piece of aluminum foil into a 2" strip you can use it to lower and raise the springform pan more easily (and safely).
As I understand it, yes. A convection oven and air fryer are essentially the same thing. I’d like to hear from other minds on this as well…

The main differences are:
1. an air fryer has the fan on top, whereas a convection oven has the fan in the back.
2. an air fryer heats much quicker, as it is much smaller. Air fryers tend to cook faster than convection ovens.

I made this today. What a cinch!!
I had some gingerbread cookies in the freezer  from Christmas. Pulsed them in the FP with some melted butter. Baked in regular oven @ 375 12 mins. I wanted a crispy crust. Continued with the recipe, as written. Added a little vanilla bean paste along with the vanilla. I wanted those little specks of vanilla bean. I made a chocolate version. I melted 4 oz. 70% cacao chocolate and 2 T cocoa powder in the condensed milk with 2 extra T milk to help keep it liquid. I have an Instant Pot with an air fryer lid and it took 35 minutes. Perfect!
I also used Oreos for the crust.

This is by far the easiest and most delicious cheesecake I've ever made! My friends loved the fluffy texture and the fact it was not too sweet. The one hint I have for the crust is to sift the graham crackers so that you have a consistent texture and it's easy to work with.

I was skeptical...really - a chase cake in the air fryer???I should have trusted Sam and crew as it was simple, quick and great tasting!

Utterly delicious. Made this last night for a dinner party and the guests raved! Super simple to make, and far less faffy than my fridge-set cheesecake recipe which requires gelatin and multiple bowls. One mixer bowl on this one and its done. We drizzled a store-ready passionfruit, mango and yuzu coulis over the top and it was excellent. This will become a go-to cheesecake recipe with multiple options for topping depending on the season.

Hi Linda,

You can, but only if you have a "true" or "European" convection oven.  Most ovens sold in the US are NOT true convection ovens,  They have only two heating elements, whereas true convection ovens add a third element, mounted near the fan, which heats the air as it is circulated directly from the fan.  I have owned both kinds and can attest that only true convection yields results equivalent to an air fryer.

title: Cuban Buñuelos With Anise Syrup Recipe
