If you can get those tubers you can try tapioca flour (thats made from yuca root, one of the listed ingredients) and the least sweet yams you can find. Anything starchy, unami, bland could potentially replace those tubers.
Tasty but very dense, and definitely needs to be dunked in the syrup. Going to try these again with baking powder or something to give them a little more spring.

title: Mexican Buñuelos With Piloncillo Syrup Recipe
author: Christina Morales

comments.commentBody: There is no syrup on Buñuelos! They are a cinnamon sugar delight! We make buñelos every year learned them from my mother. They are fragile, crispy, and sugary.

There is definitely syrup used on bunuelos, which is my favorite version, and has been, since my childhood when my (Mexican) grandmother made them for us at Christmastime.  There are other versions without syrup, too... as you describe.  Both are delicious, both are correct.  Even more variations described in the thread make me excited to try other methods!

I haven’t had traditional bunuelos - most definitely with the guava syrup - in SO LONG!  

I spent my childhood in Mexico City and remember one disastrous trip back to the States for Christmas to visit family (and introduce them to this amazing delicacy) when the jar of syrup broke in our suitcase!  My mom used my favorite pink sweater to try to mop up the mess up right there in the middle of baggage check!  I cried and cried, not for my sweater, but for the syrup!  

Thanks for the recipe!

Buñuelos like almost everything in Mexico varies by region, so syrup, no syrup will largely depend on the region. My family is from Coahuila where we rely on orange, cinnamon and maguey syrup. It's tasty but after reading this, I must try adding guava! Honestly, I don't know why I never thought of adding guava before, it sounds heavenly.
We make a version of piloncillo syrup called "miel de higo" (fig honey) by cooking several dried fig leaves in the syrup instead of the other flavorings.

The syrup with guava is heavenly!  Drizzle it on everything -- buñuelos, pancakes, vanilla ice cream or custard, tart fruit salad.  Force the fruit pulp through the sieve for the most amazing guavasauce.
My local 99Cent Only store has piloncillo cones, so think out of the box.
Traditional Mexican buñuelos certainly have a syrup made with piloncillo. That is how they are sold on the street during the Christmas holidays.
Piloncillo can be ordered from Rancho Gordo. Get some of their wonderful beans while you’re there.

I grew up in Mexico and we always use the syrup! I love this recipe.
Thank you

The texture of the two sugars is dissimilar as well; piloncillo is very hard, and brown sugar is soft and pliable. To substitute brown sugar for piloncillo, use 1 cup of dark brown sugar and 2 tablespoons molasses for each 8- to 9-ounce cone.

For easy cheater, buneulos, when I make flour tortillas, I save a few for frying instead of the griddle.  Sprinkle with cinnamon and a tiny bit of sugar or drizzle a dab of honey.

no syrup.
does this work with crocodile?
I suppose it’s eat or be eaten in Louisiana.

So excited to see this recipe! On a visit to Miami several years ago, a friend, who’s Cuban, took me to a little sandwich joint in Hialeah and they had this sandwich. I thought, “Turkey with cream cheese and… strawberries? But that is unusual enough that I bet it’s awesome.” And it was. So so good and I am going to make this ASAP.
Strawberry? No. Cranberry sauce, yes!

Actually, the cranberry sauce taste (which I enjoy) would be repellant in this sandwich. Why is it so hard for readers to try the recipe--then post comments--instead of just coming up with entirely new recipes to (cough) share.

@Juarroz: Towards the bottom of this page, at least at the time I first saw it, there is a heading that says something like "Tip: if you want, you can use leftover cranberry sauce from Thanksgiving instead of preserves."  Perhaps instead of readers "coming up with entirely new recipes to (cough) share", they are, well, reading.
Don't knock the strawberry until you've tried it. It's excellent. I've also used pickled strawberries (strawberries lightly macerated in vinegar) if you find the sweetness off-putting.

I made this just like the recipe, but with one change. I omitted the strawberry preserves (I'm not too fond of those tiny seeds) and added cherry preserves, which added a bit of tartness. Everyone liked it. The next time I made it, I put a slab of ham on there with the turkey, which is a more traditional Cuban sandwich, and it was even better. For the guy who said he disapproved of changing recipes, well, here you go.

Sadly, I can only get this sandwich when visiting the Greater Miami area--not found in all restaurants--lighter than a "Cubano" it goes well any time, particularly for a late night snack.  Some restaurants over do it with the strawberry preserves.   Flavors should balance--saltiness of cream cheese and turkey with strawberries preserves. Yes, one can experiment, but if changing more than one ingredient it is not longer Elena Ruz.
Lingonberry jam… so yummy with Turkey or chicken

Thanks for the suggestion. Hubby hates strawberries (unless they are strawberry "flavored" Pop Tart or similar. Horrors!). However, we all love lingonberries. IT seems important to balance the sweet and salt so knowing substitutes for the strawberry jam is very helpful.

Hi Gigi! 

Sorry about the difficulties with the bread. I’d recommend anything that’s slightly sweet. Besides challah, Hawaiian bread may be another substitute. The texture won’t be the same, but the sweetness goes well with the sandwich. Hope it helps!

I grew up in Miami eating Cuban food and this sandwich is one of my favorites. Thanks for the reminder. I wondered what to use since I can't get medianoche rolls where I live now. A brioche bun sounds like a good substitute.
For years my family has loved a post - Thanksgiving sandwich related to this: white sandwich bread, toasted, turkey, cream cheese, cranberry sauce, lettuce. Bacon optional.
Like 3rd cousins, thrice removed.

This recipe is great for supper on the day after Thanksgiving.  Use brioche hot dog buns and 3 1/2 ounces of turkey.  Do not overfill.  I used Neufchatel cheese and needed a little more than the suggested amount to get the 1/3 inch layer.  Strawberry preserves contains whole strawberries and can be difficult to spread evenly.  Some might prefer strawberry jam.
I would try with hot-pepper jelly !
Our fave - marionberry habanero jam with anything...

This recipe is very similar to one of my Mother's signature dishes.  In her recipe you mix the cranberry sauce and creamed cheese and add walnuts. Spread it on a croissant with turkey and lettuce.  So delicious!  But I am going to try this as written, it sounds great!
That sounds amazing!
I tried this recipe but made my own substitutions. Instead of the bread I used an everything bagel and omitted the turkey and jelly. It was really good!

@Bobby Natlos
Oh right on!

When in Miami I try to stop in the Versailles Restaurant if I have time but would never think to try their Elena Ruz Sandwich which I note is priced at $7.50. 
Since I was going to ALDI today I got their brioche hot dog buns (6 ea), thin sliced turkey breast, strawberry preserves and whipped cream cheese and made one of these sandwiches.  It was really tasty and will add it to my regular sandwich list. I spent more than $7.50 but have enough to make at least 6 sandwiches.

Omg this looks divine, made with the listed ingredients, no substitutes. It seems to all be matter of the proportions too - A bit of all but not too much of any. 
My soul needs this sandwich - now.
Thank you.

Growing up in West New York, NJ exiled in the 1960s-70s, my mom made a version of this sandwich with white bread, ham, American cheese, mayonnaise and strawberry jam. I still enjoy making it for myself from time to time. Cream cheese was served with canned guava shells on Sunday.
Could one use a Hawaiian roll for this?
One could indeed. Two could as well.

As a Cuban, I concur that while many people like the taste of cranberry, it would be a departure from the intended palate of the sandwich. There is nothing tart about it. On the other hand, substituting guava would be fairly seamless. Make your own choice and enjoy, regardless.
One word:  Lingonberries.

Wao, que rico! Liked the texture and taste of the softened and slightly warm cream cheese and strawberry spread with the ham (too bad, no turkey) and lightly browned brioche bun. And who cares if the substitutions made it a faux Elena Ruz in the eyes of the purist? This is one of those no recipe recipes.

NIce. But I'm always curious as to why a dish as classic as this (and the Cuban sandwich) is subject to alteration. In my opinion, once the strawberry jam is substituted by any other fruit preserve, the sandwich is no longer the Elena Ruz. I am all for the evolution of recipes and their improvement or customization. Just call it something else.
Lovely. Especially the buttery brioche buns crisped on the outside. Served with maduros. 10-minute dinner!

Tasty and so quick to throw together. 

If you’re like me and prefer the swine - ham it up. But I’m sure a nice savory turkey would be fab too. 

I could see making this weekly.

Tried this with a brioche hot dog bun and had a bit of a mess in the pan. Used a brioche hamburger bun on the second try and most of the spreads stayed on much better. My six (6) year old and my husband loved this. This will be on rotation for a nice cold weather lunch.
Another version: cream cheese, orange marmalade, cranberry sauce.
Yes to cranberry sauce, add orange marmalade as well. Heavenly!
yum.  i make this w/ fig jam.  and it's fab.  sweet and salty, nothing better.

This is nearly the same flavor combo as a Monte Cristo sandwich:  Mustard on one slice of bread, strawberry jam on the other.  A thin slice of cheese, turkey, and ham in the middle.  Put together and dip in egg batter.  Then fry like French Toast.  I first had a Monte Cristo in my college dorm cafeteria in the 1970s and very rarely since.  So.. the Elena Ruz sounds wonderful to me!

Monte Cristos! Had forgotten about them. There was a great restaurant (3 locations) here in Rochester who had these on the menu. They were stacked about 3+ inches high and deep fried. Served with maple syrup to dip them in. Sublime. No other place which offered them (few) could hold a candle to the ones from the "Something" Mining Co. Tried to make them at home decades ago - pale imitation. Must search NYT Cooking. They surely have a recipe or 2. Thanks for the memories/reminder!
cranberry sauce with crispy sliced apples and turkey on rye! Not sure about the cream cheese...
I used mango chutney in place of the jam. Delicious!
As written this is delicious. I also made it with roasted garlic onion jam which is even better! I used sliced brioche as I don’t have a source of the Cuban bread.

I will make this for lunch today with the leftover chicken I baked last night, brioche buns, and sliced fresh strawberries drizzled with honey because I don't have strawberry jam.   Craig Claiborne made a similar sandwich on white bread, crusts removed, with just turkey and butter, to which I like to add thinly sliced red onions.  The two kinds of sandwiches would make a nice platter for serving.

This looked so delicious but had none of the ingredients on hand. Thus these sandwiches:  on ciabatta bread, sliced deli buffalo chicken breast, plum jam, and toasted everything bagel cream cheese. The sweet/savory combo wins every time.
That sounds great!  This is what I love about so many of the NYT recipes, they can be easily adapted to what you have on hand!

This has become a lunchtime favorite in my household. It’s worth the extra effort to find medianoche rolls; I found brioche didn’t hold up quite as nicely. Strawberry jam is delicious, but occasionally I’ll use guava preserves instead to mix things up. Enjoy!
I had fig jam on hand and it was  perfect.
I remember first eating this sandwich at DB Kaplan's in Atlanta in the 80's.  It's a great post-Thanksgiving sandwich.  Any bread will work, but the key is good strawberry preserves.
Nice, but give me a turkey, cranberry sauce and stuffing sandwich on white with mayo any time.  That’s an American sandwich!
This was my mother’s favorite sandwich when she was growing up in Havana. Can confirm it is the perfect balance of sweet, salt, and a big hunk of cream cheese.
We love this sandwich, and we use a marionberry-habanero jam. Killer.

Absolutely delicious! This recipe reminds me of living in South Florida. Being far from there, I used split Brioche hot dogs rolls. Definitely a good substitute for the beloved medianoche roll. It's sweet and savory qualities are welcomed Cuban comfort food. This sandwich will be on rotation at our home.
Also yummy with turkey, cranberry sauce and a little shaved red onion
Trust, people. It is perfect.
I made it exactly as described (on a brioche hamburger bun) thinking that I would eat half. Nope!  Ate the whole thing!  This is going into the rotation!

This time I only had Rye bread but I had a craving for this sammie, always a favorite because it’s grilled cheese adjacent.  I had roasted a chicken and I subbed in Apricot preserves, something I’m on a kick for.  The essence of the recipe holds, result: delicious and craving satisfied!

what bread would you suggest if neither medianoche or brioche is available?    when i look these up, they refer to egg bread - the only way i know how to get that here is by baking at home - would prefer to buy something.   i think i would try one with strawberry preserves and one with cranberry sauce.

Made this today without meat, as I don't eat meat, but found the cream cheese and jam were still cold. I even grilled for longer than listed. That was very unpleasant. 1/3" cream cheese seemed a bit excessive. I'll try again using less cream cheese and  removing a bit of the bread from the brioche roll (to make thinner bread). I'll add meat for my meat eating husband and see what he thinks.
Lightly toasting the sandwich in the pan is only to toast the bread. The filling should be cool.
The recipe calls for "softened" cream cheese, ergo cream cheese at room temperature. My sense is that the cream cheese should not be at all cold?
My fav for years whole-wheat bread mayo avocado cold stuffing turkey cranberry sauce

The cranberry sauce tip is a terrific alternative to this bit-too-sweet use of strawberry preserves.  Make this sandwich spectacular by whipping up Melissa Clark's Cranberry Sauce recipe to add to it.
My Cuban mother used to put a teaspoon of strawberry jam in my tuna sandwiches.  Gives it another dimension of taste.

This split opinions in our house. Two of us loved it, one loved the first bite but then found it too sweet, and my daughter’s friend took one look and announced that she wouldn’t eat with us because she’d get in trouble with her mother. I think the brioche hot dog rolls I used were too narrow, so everything got a bit messy rather than crispy. Will just use brioche next time and a light hand with the preserves. But there will definitely be a next time.
I made these slider size with brioche rolls, and sliced off the top of the bun before toasting. Nice for a snack.

Thank you for the cranberry sauce suggestion! I like this with tunafish. Don't knock it til you try it. You know when you're at a wedding and they have those sandwiches with the crusts cut off? And everyone's like "is this turkey or chicken?" or "is this chicken or tuna?" No one can tell the difference, that's why cranberry sauce goes with all three! But the best of all is tunafish and cranberry sauce. btw (by the way), they have strawberry cream cheese too. I bet that would be great.
Also delicious on Hawaiian Rolls
Delicious.  Thanks for this simple way to use up leftover turkey.
Unusually good.

I made this as written on brioche sliders side by side with ones where I subbed jellied cranberry sauce. Even with just a little strawberry jam we found the original too sweet. The combo was a little jarring at first bite which was surprising because I love salty/sweet. To be fair, I find most jams too sweet for my taste so I'm sure it's me.
Not to say I'd never have it again but I've loved the cranberry version for years so that's probably going to be my first choice. Thank you for sharing.

Please, dismiss “ the tip” at the end of  this recipe.  Do not substitute the strawberry preserves with leftover cranberry sauce!!!  The combination of strawbery preserves and cream cheese in the Elena Ruz is what makes this sandwich unique and delicious.  No substitutes, please!
Loved it!! Very tasty.

I made it more contemporary by using grape jelly, spam and blue cheese spread on a croissant to ad a french accent to the dish. You could call it American in Paris visits Cuba. Gives it a nice vintage feel imo.
Used sharp Vermont cheddar, Hawaiian rolls, cranberry compote, and smoked thanksgiving turkey. Delicious.  But that not that wasn’t the recipe…more like inspired by the recipe
If only it was possible to get real Cuban bread outside of Tampa and Miami (sort of).
I've had "Cuban" sandwiches before, I like them a lot actually. Usually on Focaccia bread, pork, mustard and pickles is the basic. This one seems an odd combination to me.
Never been to Cuba, but ate this delightful sandwich in New Zealand.
